findings.title: Committed source memo-20260527 not marked as superseded
findings.location: Memos/memo-20260527-operator-credential-models.md:entire document
findings.issue: The citizen-capability model deliberately supersedes three key assumptions from memo-20260527 (one-SA-per-role → one-SA-per-person; role-keyed verbs → identity-keyed verbs; verb bodies stay separate → verb bodies converge in shape). The citizen memo correctly documents these divergences (lines 227-240) and the paddock directs readers to update memo-20260527 when frozen (line 22-23). However, memo-20260527 itself carries no forward reference, marker, or note indicating it is superseded. Future readers may treat it as the current shape and miss the structural changes.
findings.evidence: Citizen-capability memo lines 227-240: 'memo-20260527 states the operator verbs "keep their names, only their bodies differ," and that keyfile holds "one SA key file each per role." This heat supersedes both.' Paddock line 20-23: 'NOTE — supersedes memo-20260527 on one point... Update that memo when the model is frozen.' The committed memo-20260527 contains no backward reference to indicate it is superseded.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an explicit note to memo-20260527 (after the Status line or at the end of the document) stating: 'NOTE (2026-06-05): This memo describes the initial two-tier plan. The keyfile-tier shape has been revised in memo-20260605-citizen-capability-model.md, which supersedes the assumptions in the Operator-admin verbs section (one-SA-per-role is now one-SA-per-person, and verb bodies now converge structurally rather than just in name). Consult the citizen-capability memo and its paddock for the revised model.'

findings.title: Federation verb compatibility assumption unstated
findings.location: Memos/memo-20260605-citizen-capability-model.md:lines 14-15, 36-38
findings.issue: The memo claims the citizen model's capability layer is identical to the federation tier's, and states only identity-kind and token mint differ. The spec docs (RBSDK-director_invest, RBSDD-director_divest, RBSRK-retriever_invest) all reference `serviceAccount:…` member strings in grant/revoke/roster. The memo does not explicitly verify that these verbs will work unchanged with `principal://…` member strings under federation, despite claiming 'structural isomorphism.' This is a load-bearing assumption for the federation enablement claim.
findings.evidence: Citizen memo line 36: 'only identity-kind, that handle, and the token mint (signed-JWT vs STS) differ.' RBSDK lines 109-113: 'Add `serviceAccount:«DIRECTOR_EMAIL»` with roles:...' RBSDD lines 35-46: 'Remove `serviceAccount:«SA_EMAIL»`...' RBSRK lines 107-108: 'Add `serviceAccount:«RETRIEVER_EMAIL»`...' No federation spec exists yet to verify this assumption holds.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a verification subsection under the 'Structural isomorphism' paragraph (after line 38) stating: 'Verification: the grant/revoke/roster verbs operate only on member strings (e.g., `serviceAccount:…`, future `principal://…`); role and bindings are decoupled. Federation implementation must confirm that IAM policy read/write operations accept `principal://` subjects identically to `serviceAccount:` subjects in the binding member field. This assumption is not yet tested.'

findings.title: Roster-write authority security boundary insufficiently specified
findings.location: Memos/memo-20260605-citizen-capability-model.md:lines 88-122
findings.issue: The memo correctly identifies that roster-write authority must be 'at least as protected as the grant verbs' (lines 119-122) and calls this a 'security boundary, not a storage convenience' (line 122). However, the memo defers the physical home (GCS vs GAR) and explicit ACL choice as 'open' (lines 88-92, paddock line 143). This leaves the security invariant stated but not bounded: what is the minimum acceptable ACL for roster-write? The memo should state this invariant precisely before deferring the storage choice.
findings.evidence: Citizen memo lines 119-122: 'auto-converging deficits treats the declared roster as authoritative intent, so writing the roster *upward* is itself a grant. The auto-heal is safe only if roster-write authority is at least as protected as the grant verbs (setIamPolicy); otherwise it is a softer escalation path than setIamPolicy.' Lines 88-92: 'Physical home (open). A GCS object in the depot bucket... Decide in-heat — but the home must place roster-write authority at least on par with grant authority... so this is a security decision, not only storage.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a paragraph after line 122 stating: 'Roster-write access control minimum: roster-write must require authentication equivalent to a `setIamPolicy` call on the same resource scope (project or resource-level, depending on final storage location). The mechanism differs by storage (GCS bucket policy vs GAR artifact policy vs RBRD tripwire), but the protection bar is invariant. Any storage choice must satisfy this before the heat closes.'

findings.title: SA tags (Preview) labeled 'proper' despite stability risk
findings.location: Memos/memo-20260605-citizen-capability-model.md:lines 217-218, 140-144
findings.issue: The memo asserts that SA tags are 'the proper metadata mechanism' for the orphan marker (line 141-142), then immediately offers a 'stable fallback' (the description sentinel, lines 143-144). This framing understates the risk: SA tags are in Preview status and may change. Calling a Preview feature 'proper' while offering a fallback creates ambiguity about which is the intended primary path and which is secondary. The memo should clarify whether Preview-ness is acceptable or if the description sentinel is the current baseline.
findings.evidence: Citizen memo lines 217-218: 'Service accounts do not support resource labels. They support **tags** (tag bindings), currently in Preview. So the orphan marker uses tags (Preview) or the description sentinel, not a label.' Lines 141-142: 'Marker options: **SA tags** (tag bindings): key-value, listable, condition-usable — the proper metadata mechanism. Caveat: SA tags are **Preview** (may change).'
findings.suggested_fix: Revise lines 141-144 to: '**SA tags** (tag bindings): key-value, listable, condition-usable — preferred when stabilized. Status: currently Preview (may change); production risk. **SA `description` sentinel**: stable fallback, used only for the orphan-sweep; the roster owns capability, the description earns exactly this one job. Current recommendation: use the description sentinel until SA tags exit Preview.'

findings.title: Authority topology section does not flag unresolved keyfile-governor choice at point of discussion
findings.location: Memos/memo-20260605-citizen-capability-model.md:lines 149-184
findings.issue: The authority topology section (lines 149-184) discusses whether a standing governor 'buys nothing' in keyfile mode and describes it as 'pure attack surface' (line 174-175). The section then defers the choice ('drop or demote it') to an open decision (paddock line 145). Readers may not realize that the entire premise — whether a standing governor is advisable in keyfile mode — is unfrozen. The memo should flag the open nature of this choice at the point where it is discussed, not only in the separate 'open' section of the paddock.
findings.evidence: Citizen memo lines 173-176: 'Keyfile/solo (payor == admin) → topology: a standing owner-holding citizen buys nothing and is pure attack surface; drop or demote it.' Paddock line 145: 'Whether the keyfile tier drops the standing governor entirely or demotes it to non-privileged.' No signal in the authority section itself that this is open.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a NOTE after line 176: 'NOTE (open): Whether the keyfile tier drops the standing governor entirely or demotes it to non-privileged is deferred to heat scoping. The analysis above assumes dropping or demotion; federate-tier analysis may differ. See paddock open items.'

findings.title: Class-C propagation correctness deferred to rbk-08 addendum not cited
findings.location: Memos/memo-20260605-citizen-capability-model.md:lines 108-116
findings.issue: The asymmetric healing section (lines 108-116) discusses auto-convergence of deficits and mentions 'logged, never silent' but does not cite the Class-C addendum to memo-20260604-credential-churn-leak-and-propagation-races (lines 143-176 of that memo), which corrects the propagation model for both grant and revoke paths. The governor-mantle memo correctly references this (line 64), but the citizen memo discusses healing without citing the propagation-tolerance requirement for the revoke path, which implementers will need.
findings.evidence: Citizen memo lines 110-116: 'Deficit is ours (our own grant failed to complete) → auto-converge up to D (re-run the idempotent grant), logged, never silent.' Governor-mantle memo line 64: 'RBSCIP Class-C declaration generalized to grant+revoke'. Memo-20260604 addendum (lines 143-176): documents the symmetric Class-C requirement for revoke-path reads.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a citation after line 111: '(See memo-20260604-credential-churn-leak-and-propagation-races, Addendum, for the Class-C propagation tolerance requirement that applies to both grant and revoke resource-scope reads.)'

findings.title: "citizen" collides head-on with RBSHR's existing "citizen" — and inverts its meaning
findings.location: memo §Scope and vocabulary (line 18, 27) vs RBSHR-HorizonRoadmap.adoc lines 97-99 and 222
findings.issue: The memo mints "citizen" = a keyfile-tier operator IDENTITY (a person; a depot-issued SA holding an RBRA key). But RBSHR already uses "citizen" to mean a depot-resident ARTIFACT — i.e. property, the exact opposite category. RBSHR hoard entry: "A {rbtgi_depot} citizen alongside the {rbtga_ark}" and bullion entry: "bullions are the new AWS-side citizen with S3-native retention semantics." Worse, the memo's OTHER new term, "holdings," is defined (line 27) as "the depot's artifacts (arks/hoards/bullions) ... Property, not identities" — which is precisely what RBSHR currently calls citizens. So the heat simultaneously (a) reuses "citizen" for the opposite category and (b) mints "holdings" to mean the thing "citizen" already meant. This is a within-domain monosemy violation of the worst kind: a person/property inversion on a live term, guaranteed to produce pattern-recognition collisions (CLAUDE.md Quoin Sub-Letter Discipline, AXLA/MIND semantic-uniqueness sensibilities).
findings.evidence: RBSHR-HorizonRoadmap.adoc:97 "A {rbtgi_depot}\ncitizen alongside the {rbtga_ark}"; RBSHR-HorizonRoadmap.adoc:222 "bullions are the new AWS-side citizen"; memo line 27 "Holdings — the depot's artifacts (arks/hoards/bullions). Property, not identities."
findings.suggested_fix: Resolve before the heat freezes, two clean options: (1) keep "citizen" for the operator identity and rename RBSHR's two artifact uses to "holdings" (the term the memo already defines for them) — a 2-line RBSHR edit that also removes RBSHR's own ark/hoard/bullion "citizen" overload; or (2) pick a different identity noun and leave RBSHR untouched. Either way the memo must add an explicit "supersedes RBSHR 'citizen' (artifact) usage" note paralleling its memo-20260527 divergence note, since RBSHR is a committed spec. Do not leave "citizen" meaning property in one spec and person in another.

findings.title: "declared roster" overloads the existing `roster` cult-verb, which reads ACTUAL IAM facts, not declared intent
findings.location: memo §The declared roster + §Verb dissolution table (line 50) vs RBSDR-director_roster.adoc
findings.issue: The memo mints "declared roster" = depot-resident DATA of intended capability-set memberships (identity -> {sets held}). But RBK already has a `roster` cult-verb (RBSDR) whose whole job is to emit the ACTUAL discovered state — "rosters {rbtr_director} service accounts via per-SA fact emission — one roster-director fact file per discovered identity." The new noun (declared/intended) and the existing verb (discovered/actual) are opposite poles of the very intent-vs-actual axis the audit section is built around — yet the verb-dissolution table maps "roster | read the declared roster" (line 50), silently retargeting the existing fact-reading verb onto the new intent noun without flagging the meaning flip. memo-20260527 and RBSHR add a third sense ("participant roster" / "role-to-identity roster"). One word now spans declared-intent, discovered-actual, and the verb that produces the actual — a monosemy failure inside one domain.
findings.evidence: RBSDR-director_roster.adoc:59 "rosters {rbtr_director} service accounts via per-SA fact emission — one roster-director fact file per discovered identity"; memo line 50 "| roster | read the declared roster |"; memo §The audit contrasts "declared" vs "actual".
findings.suggested_fix: Distinguish the two senses explicitly. Either rename the intent store (e.g. "capability ledger" / "declared holdings ledger") and reserve "roster" for the actual-state read, or rename the existing read verb. At minimum, add one sentence to §Verb dissolution stating that the old `roster` (emits actual IAM facts) and the new "read the declared roster" are different operations on opposite poles, and that the audit is precisely the diff between them — otherwise a reader conflates the verb that produces 'actual' with the noun that holds 'declared.'

findings.title: No code-prefix mint reserved for the citizen / capability-set / declared-roster machinery, despite persistent identifiers being introduced
findings.location: memo §The declared roster (Physical home, line 88-92) and §Orphan hazard (line 142-144)
findings.issue: The minting discipline (CLAUDE.md "Minting Workflow" + "Extended Namespace Checklist") requires enumerating every namespace a new persistent name touches BEFORE the heat introduces it. This heat introduces several persistent identifiers with no reserved code prefix: a GCS object holding the declared roster (its object key/path is a persistent name), capability-set definition constants lifted out of invest bodies, the SA-description sentinel value, and (per the verb table) new commands grant/revoke/rekey/add-citizen. The memo names none of these in `rb`-prefixed code terms and reserves no module letter. Separately, the orphan sentinel is given as the literal string `rbk:…` — but `rbk` is the KIT directory prefix (Tools/rbk/), not a free code identifier; a bare `rbk:` sentinel is acceptable as a marker literal but the memo should say so and confirm it is intentionally the kit token, not an un-minted new prefix.
findings.evidence: memo line 88 "A GCS object in the depot bucket" (unnamed key); line 143 "SA `description` sentinel (`rbk:…`)"; CLAUDE.md "Minting Workflow ... Enumerate namespaces — list every place this name will appear (code, refs, commands, env vars, target paths)".
findings.suggested_fix: Add a short "Mints (open, settle in-heat)" subsection enumerating the namespaces the model touches — roster GCS object key, capability-set definition constants, new verb commands, description-sentinel literal — and either reserve a code prefix or explicitly defer it as a named open. Note that the `rbk:` sentinel is deliberately the kit token, not a new prefix allocation. This is the namespace-enumeration step, distinct from the (correctly) deferred scope/heat-chop decision.

findings.title: "federate" as identity noun sits next to RBSHR's "federates" verb (envoy/Workload sense) — disambiguate
findings.location: memo §Scope and vocabulary (line 19) vs RBSHR-HorizonRoadmap.adoc lines 213, 238
findings.issue: The peer noun "federate" (future federation-tier OPERATOR identity, Workforce/`principal://`) reads cleanly against memo-20260527, but RBSHR already uses "federates" as a VERB for the envoy — a different actor in a different product: "it federates to AWS via OIDC Workload Identity Federation" (line 213), "federates to the embassy's IAM role" (line 238). memo-20260527 itself stresses Workforce (operator) vs Workload (envoy) are two distinct products that "never chain for a single actor." So "federate"(noun, operator, Workforce) and "federates"(verb, envoy, Workload) live one grep apart and risk being read as the same family. The noun choice is defensible; the collision is only that the word-stem is now load-bearing on two sides of the very Workforce/Workload seam the project takes pains to separate.
findings.evidence: memo line 19 "Federate (future...) — a federation-tier operator identity: an external-IdP principal:// subject"; RBSHR-HorizonRoadmap.adoc:213 "it federates to AWS via OIDC Workload Identity Federation"; memo-20260527 §Workforce vs Workload "they coexist but never chain for a single actor."
findings.suggested_fix: Keep "federate" but add a one-line disambiguation in §Scope: the federate (noun) is a Workforce-federated OPERATOR; it is unrelated to the envoy's Workload "federates to AWS" (RBSHR), which is machine egress. This pins the term to the operator/Workforce side and pre-empts the cross-product conflation.

result.reasoning: I read the cited memo and all three cited specs independently. The finding's factual premises hold: RBSDK (lines 109, 130, 140, 162), RBSDD (lines 35, 41, 44), and RBSRK (line 107) all use `serviceAccount:«…EMAIL»` member strings in grant/revoke, and the memo does assert structural isomorphism. But the finding's central thesis — that the member-string compatibility is "unstated" and "not explicitly verified" — is directly contradicted by the memo's own text. Lines 35-38 say the isomorphism holds "modulo the opaque principal handle the identity layer supplies (`serviceAccount:…` vs `principal://…`); only identity-kind, that handle, and the token mint differ." That is exactly the seam the finding wants named, and the memo makes it the single most-emphasized difference between tiers, reinforced at lines 59-60 ("Capability verbs (grant/revoke) never branch on tier — both are an IAM binding on a pre-existing identity") and line 54 ("Actor and capability-set are parameters"). The proposed fix is essentially a paraphrase of lines 35-38. The only residual nuance — that the memo doesn't explicitly tag GCP's acceptance of `principal://` in member fields as an unsurveyed external (Pale) assumption — is largely absorbed by the memo's pervasive "Nothing here is built yet" / "federate (future, not this heat)" framing, since federation is deferred and the model is correctly conditional. That residual is a low-value nuance, not a medium clarity defect. The finding mischaracterizes the most-stated seam in the memo as unstated.
result.refined_fix: No substantive fix needed; the compatibility seam is already explicit at memo lines 35-38 (`serviceAccount:…` vs `principal://…` as the one modulo) and lines 59-60. If anything, a single low-value clause could be appended at line 38 flagging that the federate side is an unsurveyed Pale assumption: "(the federate member-string form is presumed but untested against live IAM, since federation is out of scope this heat)." This is optional polish, not a real gap — the memo's not-built-yet/future-tier framing already conveys it.

findings.title: Governor mantle revoke-before-delete gap
findings.location: rbgp_Payor.sh:1748
findings.issue: The governor_mantle function deletes existing governor-* service accounts without first revoking their roles/owner binding. This creates deleted:…?uid= tombstones on the project policy, accumulating without bound across re-mantles. The memo (lines 204-206) explicitly specifies this should be fixed with 'the generic revoke-before-delete', reusing the rbk-08 revoke layer, but the code contains no revoke call in this path.
findings.evidence: Code line 1748 (rbuh_json DELETE) deletes each governor-* SA directly. Memo-20260605-citizen-capability-model.md lines 204-206: 'The leak closes cheaply: add the generic revoke-before-delete (reusing the rbk-08 revoke layer + Class-C propagation tolerance) before the existing delete — no tombstone, no naming change.' See also memo-20260605-governor-mantle-tombstone-leak.md documenting 8 observed tombstones on the canonical depot.
findings.suggested_fix: Before the DELETE at rbgp_Payor.sh:1748, call rbgi_revoke_project_member for roles/owner (and any other established grants) on the outgoing governor SA. This is project-scoped and caller (Payor) is the project owner, so no Class-C retry tolerance is needed (unlike resource-scope revokes on director/retriever).

findings.title: Scoping ambiguity: role-keyed vs identity-keyed accessor is unresolved
findings.location: memo-20260605-citizen-capability-model.md:237-238
findings.issue: The memo (lines 237-238) explicitly states an open scoping question: 'Whether the heat's load-bearing core is the role-keyed no-behavior stepping-stone or the identity-keyed end state is an open scoping question — see the paddock.' The paddock is undefined (per user statement). Without this resolution, the design's intent on whether this heat delivers one-SA-per-person (memo line 187, the stated goal) or role-keyed status-quo (the implied current state per code inspection) is unclear. This is load-bearing: it affects the token accessor architecture, the RBRA file path structure, and the migration cost.
findings.evidence: Memo lines 187-191 define 'One citizen per person = one SA' as the end-state goal. Memo lines 233-238 describe the divergence: '20260527's single code seam is a role-keyed token accessor… Under one-SA-per-person the accessor becomes identity-keyed… (Whether the heat's load-bearing core is the role-keyed no-behavior stepping-stone or the identity-keyed end state is an open scoping question — see the paddock.)' Current code (rbgg_Governor.sh line 658) implements director-<identity>, which is role-keyed (one SA per role per identity).
findings.suggested_fix: Clarify in the paddock whether this heat's MVP is (a) role-keyed, no-behavior-change (status quo with capability decoupling), or (b) identity-keyed, one-SA-per-person (the long-term goal). If (a), update the memo's 'This heat supersedes both' claim (line 230-231) to clarify it's incremental, not a full supersession. If (b), document the migration hazard and token accessor refactoring scope. Anchor the clarification in the paddock's stated scope section.

findings.title: Memo claims identity verb decoupling (establish/rekey/remove) but code is still fused
findings.location: memo-20260604-credential-churn-leak-and-propagation-races.md:82-94; vs rbgg_Governor.sh:612-616
findings.issue: Memo-20260604 (the H1/H2 fix spec, which memo-20260605 references as an enabler at line 206) specifies decomposing the fused invest into: (1) Establish = ensure SA exists and grant bindings once, idempotent; (2) Re-key = rotate key only, presumes established; (3) Remove = revoke all bindings, then delete SA. However, the code still implements the old fused model: zrbgg_create_service_account_with_key (lines 159-364) does SA-create + binding-grant + key-mint all together, and divest has no revoke. The memo (line 206) claims this is an 'enabler', but if the split is not implemented, the governor tombstone fix is incomplete.
findings.evidence: Memo-20260604 lines 82-94: 'Stop fusing. Split the one invest into two operations: Establish (one-time per SA… grant its Bindings once. Idempotent…), Re-key (every issuance: rotate the Key only), Teardown (rare… revoke Bindings, then delete the SA).' Code rbgg_Governor.sh:zrbgg_create_service_account_with_key still executes SA-create (if absent) + binding-grant + key-mint atomically. Memo-20260605 line 206 states the mantle fix 'reusing the rbk-08 revoke layer' which is the H1 fix, implying H1 decomposition should be in place, but the code shows no establish/rekey/remove boundary.
findings.suggested_fix: Clarify the dependency: Is the governor revoke-before-delete fix (finding 1) a standalone targeted fix requiring no decomposition, OR is it contingent on the H1 establish/rekey/remove refactoring? If standalone, document the fix in rbgp_governor_mantle as a narrowly-scoped revoke-then-delete. If contingent, update the paddock to scope the establish/rekey/remove decomposition as a prerequisite, and document the implementation phase.

findings.title: Memo claims 'one SA per person' but code implements 'director/retriever per role per identity'
findings.location: memo-20260605-citizen-capability-model.md:230-231 vs RBSDK-director_invest.adoc:12
findings.issue: Memo lines 230-231 state 'the citizen tier is one SA *per person*, not per role.' However, the spec RBSDK line 12 documents the current design as 'the SA account name is composed as `${RBCC_account_director}-<identity>`' where identity is operator-supplied, and the code (rbgg_Governor.sh line 658) implements exactly that. This means there is one director SA per (identity), one retriever SA per (identity), etc. — still role-keyed, not person-keyed. The memo's claim at line 230-231 is either aspirational (future end-state) or incorrectly asserts the current state as the new design.
findings.evidence: Memo line 230-231: 'This heat supersedes both: the verb bodies converge in shape (not just names), and the citizen tier is one SA *per person*, not per role.' RBSDK line 12: 'the SA account name is composed as `${RBCC_account_director}-<identity>`'. Code rbgg_Governor.sh line 658: `local z_account_name="${RBCC_account_director}-${z_identity}"`. This creates director-alice, director-bob, retriever-alice, retriever-bob — four SAs for two people, organized by role.
findings.suggested_fix: Either: (a) If this heat DOES deliver one-SA-per-person, update the code and specs to use identity-only SA names (e.g., director-alice holds both director and retriever roles), and update the investment/divest paths to grant multiple capability-sets on one SA. (b) If this heat DOES NOT deliver one-SA-per-person, correct the memo to clarify that the 'supersession' is on the capability decoupling layer only (grant/revoke/roster verbatim across tiers), not on the identity layer (which remains role-keyed in this heat). Add a forward-reference to a future heat for the per-person migration.

findings.title: Orphan marker (SA description sentinel) not yet implemented in code
findings.location: memo-20260605-citizen-capability-model.md:144; vs rbgg_Governor.sh:159-364
findings.issue: The memo specifies at line 143-144 that SA identity markers (to catch orphaned failed-add SAs) should use 'SA tags (Preview) or the description sentinel, not a label.' The code creates SAs with displayName and description at rbgg_Governor.sh lines 198-199, but does not set any `rbk:…` description sentinel value to mark RBK-managed SAs for orphan sweeps. Without this, a failed add-citizen (SA created, but credential delivery fails) would create an untrackable SA.
findings.evidence: Memo lines 142-144: 'Marker options: SA tags (tag bindings): key-value, listable, condition-usable — the proper metadata mechanism. Caveat: SA tags are **Preview** (may change). SA `description` sentinel (`rbk:…`): stable fallback, used only for the orphan-sweep; the roster owns capability, the description earns exactly this one job.' Code rbgg_Governor.sh:197-199 sets displayName but no description sentinel (description is set to operational text only, not an `rbk:` marker).
findings.suggested_fix: Add an `rbk:` sentinel to the description field when creating director/retriever/governor SAs (e.g., prepend 'rbk:managed ' or include 'rbk=<identity>' in the description). Document the sentinel pattern and implement an orphan-sweep verb that lists all SAs matching the sentinel in the description. This is citizen-tier-specific and a separate concern from the hot-path credential grant/revoke.

findings.title: Spec RBSDK claims identity-keyed composition, but code shows role-keyed
findings.location: RBSDK-director_invest.adoc:30 vs rbgg_Governor.sh:658
findings.issue: RBSDK line 30 documents the SA composition as `${RBCC_account_director}-<identity>`, which is a role-keyed pattern (the role name 'director' is hardcoded). The operation is parameterized by identity (param1), not by both role and identity. This is consistent with the code but conflicts with the memo's claim that this heat delivers 'one SA per person' (which would require dropping the role name from the SA ID).
findings.evidence: RBSDK line 30: '`{{rbbc_store}} {{rbbc_account_name}} as `${RBCC_account_director}-<identity>` (operator-supplied identity composing the account name)'. Code rbgg_Governor.sh:658: `local z_account_name="${RBCC_account_director}-${z_identity}"`. Both hard-code the role (director).
findings.suggested_fix: No fix needed if this is intentional role-keyed design for this heat. Instead, clarify the memo (line 230-231) to state 'one SA per [role, identity] pair' to be precise. If moving to one-SA-per-person is a future heat, document that transition as a separate effort and link from the paddock.

result.reasoning:
I read the cited memo lines directly. The finding's central claim — that the security invariant is "stated but not bounded" — is contradicted by the memo's own text, which the finding itself quotes. Line 119-120 states the bound precisely: roster-write must be "at least as protected as the grant verbs (setIamPolicy); otherwise it is a softer escalation path than setIamPolicy." That IS a concrete minimum bar, pegged to a named, storage-independent mechanism (setIamPolicy). I verified via RBSDK-director_invest.adoc that the grant/invest verbs are indeed governor-driven IAM-policy writes, so pegging the bar to setIamPolicy is accurate, not vague.

The finding's suggested fix is a near-verbatim restatement of the already-present line 120 ("must require authentication equivalent to a setIamPolicy call"), with one addition: "on the same resource scope." But the resource-scope-vs-project-scope dimension is already developed extensively elsewhere in the memo — the entire Authority section (lines 154-172) distinguishes project-scoped grants (IAM-limitable via modifiedGrantsByRole) from resource-scoped grants (repo/bucket/SA, not IAM-limitable, covered by detective audit). The fix adds nothing the memo lacks.

Deferring the physical home (GCS vs GAR vs RBRD) while stating the invariant is the correct ordering, not a gap: line 88-92 already says the home "must place roster-write authority at least on par with grant authority... so this is a security decision, not only storage." The invariant (parity with setIamPolicy) is precisely the storage-independent bar the finding asks for; the ACL mechanism necessarily differs by storage (GCS bucket policy vs GAR vs RBRD tripwire) and cannot be fixed before the home is chosen. The finding internally contradicts itself: it quotes the bounding sentence in its evidence, then claims the invariant is unbounded.
result.refined_fix: No fix needed. The memo already states the bound precisely at lines 119-120 ("roster-write authority is at least as protected as the grant verbs (setIamPolicy)") — a concrete, storage-independent minimum pegged to the exact mechanism the grant verbs use (confirmed governor-driven setIamPolicy writes in RBSDK-director_invest.adoc). The resource-scope nuance the suggested fix adds is already covered in the Authority section (lines 154-172, project-scope vs resource-scope split with modifiedGrantsByRole limits and detective-audit residual). Deferring the physical home while holding the invariant fixed is correct ordering, since the concrete ACL mechanism is storage-dependent and the invariant is not.

result.reasoning:
I read the cited memo sections (lines 136-147, 214-225) in full and web-verified the Preview claim against Google's own docs.

The Preview fact is accurate: docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/service-accounts-tags explicitly states service-account tags are in Preview ('Pre-GA Offerings Terms'). So the memo's factual representation is correct — no error there.

The finding's actual claim is a clarity/framing defect: that calling a Preview feature 'proper' while offering a 'stable fallback' creates ambiguity about the intended primary path. This does not hold up against the text:

1. The two phrases describe orthogonal axes, not a contradiction. 'The proper metadata mechanism' describes *semantic fitness* — tags are the correct GCP construct for metadata, vs. abusing the description field. 'Preview (may change)' is the *stability* caveat, stated immediately and explicitly on the same line. 'Stable fallback' is the pragmatic alternative. A reader is given both options with their tradeoffs plainly attached; nothing is understated or hidden. This is exactly the Pale/membrane discipline the project's own ROE prescribes (characterize the foreign behavior, name the bend).

2. The block is explicitly an option enumeration — line 139 reads 'Marker options:'. It lists candidates; it does not (and at this stage should not) declare a winner.

3. The finding's suggested fix would inject 'Current recommendation: use the description sentinel until SA tags exit Preview.' That is precisely a mechanism-selection / scoping decision, which the task brief states is INTENTIONALLY deferred and must not be filed as a finding. The fix pre-empts an open heat decision.

So the factual underpinning checks out but the alleged clarity defect is a misreading ('proper' taken as 'production-ready' rather than 'semantically correct'), and the proposed fix is wrong in direction. This is a low-value stylistic quibble, not a real issue.
result.refined_fix: No change needed. The memo already presents the SA-tags-vs-description tradeoff correctly: tags are the semantically proper metadata construct, flagged immediately as Preview/may-change; the description sentinel is the stable fallback. Both options and their tradeoffs are explicit under an explicit 'Marker options:' enumeration. If any micro-polish were wanted, it would be to change 'the proper metadata mechanism' to 'the semantically proper metadata mechanism' to foreclose a 'proper == production-ready' misread — but this is cosmetic, not load-bearing. Do NOT add a 'current recommendation' selecting one marker: that is the deferred mechanism-selection decision the heat intentionally holds open.

findings.title: Table 'Verb dissolution' could mislead on roster current behavior
findings.location: memo-20260605-citizen-capability-model.md § Verb dissolution, line 50
findings.issue: The table entry 'roster | read the declared roster' describes the model's future behavior but appears in a table labeled 'Today' in the first column. Readers may misinterpret this as describing current behavior. In reality, the current RBSDR spec derives the roster from IAM by filtering service accounts on name regex, not from a separate declared-roster data structure. The memo does explain this later (lines 72–73, referencing the 'retired derive from IAM' approach), but the connection is not obvious from the table alone.
findings.evidence: Table at lines 45–52 labels first column 'Today'; line 50 shows 'roster | read the declared roster' without forward-looking marker. Contrast with RBSDR spec (lines 36–45) which filters `«ACCOUNTS»` by regex against the composed email pattern, reading from IAM. Memo lines 72–73 state 'the retired "derive from IAM" cinch was self-defeating...' indicating the shift from current (IAM-derived) to proposed (declared-roster) behavior.
findings.suggested_fix: Clarify the table entry 'roster | read the declared roster' by appending a note like '(proposed)' or '(under model)' to distinguish forward-looking design from current behavior. Alternatively, add a table header note: 'Column 2 describes the model's proposed decomposition; current implementation may differ (e.g., roster is currently IAM-derived).'

findings.title: SA-naming migration (role-prefix removal) is the load-bearing precondition the memo never specifies
findings.location: memo §Blast radius / §The declared roster; vs RBSDK/RBSRK §Compose ... Identity, RBSDR §Filter, RBSDD §Synthesize Email, RBSRR
findings.issue: The whole model rests on the SA name becoming identity-only (e.g. `alice`), but the memo never names that change or its downstream spec edits. The blast-radius claim ‘one citizen per person = one SA = one key carrying the union’ is only achievable if the SA is named for the person, not the role: GCP cannot give one person a single SA while the naming scheme is `director-alice` + `retriever-alice` (those are two distinct SAs = two keys = exactly the old model the memo says it discards). The roster section even argues from this premise ('once the role prefix leaves the identity name', 'Without a name prefix, deriving the roster from IAM is lossy') — so the memo KNOWS the prefix departs, yet treats the departure as already-done rather than as work. Meanwhile every current identity verb hard-encodes the role prefix: RBSDK/RBSRK compose `${RBCC_account_director}-<identity>`; RBSDR filters and strips `^${RBCC_account_director}-(.+)@...$`; RBSDD synthesizes `${RBCC_account_director}-<identity>@...`; RBSRR defines three role-keyed RBRA paths (director/retriever/governor). None of these survive the identity-only naming the model presumes, and none appear in the memo's 'what done looks like'. This is the single most valuable missing piece: it is the foundation both the one-SA-per-person claim and the lossy-IAM-roster justification stand on.
findings.evidence: memo:187 'One citizen per person = one SA = one key carrying the **union**'; memo:73 'once the role prefix leaves the identity name'; memo:76 'Without a name prefix, deriving the roster from IAM is lossy'. RBSDK:30 `${RBCC_account_director}-<identity>`; RBSDR:37 `^${RBCC_account_director}-(.+)@${RBGD_SA_EMAIL_FULL}$`; RBSDD:28 `${RBCC_account_director}-<identity>@${RBGD_SA_EMAIL_FULL}`; RBSRR:43,51,59 three role-keyed rbra_file paths.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a dedicated section to the memo making the SA-naming migration explicit and enumerating the concrete spec/regime surfaces it touches: (1) SA name becomes identity-only; account-name composition in RBSDK/RBSRK drops the role prefix; (2) RBSDR roster-by-IAM-prefix-filter is retired (consistent with the memo's own 'roster from IAM is lossy' point) and replaced by the declared-roster read; (3) RBSDD email synthesis becomes identity-only; (4) RBSRR's three role-keyed RBRA paths collapse to one identity-keyed path. State the migration of existing `director-*`/`retriever-*` SAs (rename is impossible in GCP — it is delete-old + establish-new-identity-SA, which re-enters the create/grant DRS race and the delete/recreate flap from memo-20260604).

findings.title: Audit surplus-detection has no exception for Mason's legitimate verb-placed bindings
findings.location: memo §The audit ('Audit domain = roster-identities × set-defined bindings; non-roster principals are out of scope') vs §Scope ('cloud-native system identities (Mason...) ... reusing the capability-set abstraction')
findings.issue: The memo says cloud-native system identities (Mason, future Envoy) 'sit outside' the civic ontology but 'reuse the capability-set abstraction without the citizen lifecycle,' and separately says the audit domain excludes 'non-roster principals.' These two statements are unreconciled, and the gap is operationally live: Mason holds five-plus standing RBK bindings placed by depot_levy (artifactregistry.writer, storage.objectViewer, viewer, logging.logWriter, plus tokenCreator on the CB agent) AND its repo writer binding is re-asserted inside director_invest's complete-policy-write. If Mason is a non-roster principal, the audit either (a) silently ignores a whole class of RBK-placed bindings — a blind spot in the very drift signal the audit exists to provide — or (b) if Mason 'reuses capability-sets', its bindings ARE set-defined and the audit must place Mason in some roster-like home, which the model has not designed. As written, an audit that compares declared-roster identities against actual RBK-relevant bindings will read every Mason binding as surplus-fragment (bindings belonging to no whole declared set) unless a permanent carve-out exists. No such carve-out is specified.
findings.evidence: memo:134 'Audit domain = roster-identities × set-defined bindings; non-roster principals are out of scope.'; memo:29-31 'cloud-native system identities (Mason, future Envoy) sit outside it, reusing the capability-set abstraction without the citizen lifecycle'. RBSDE:192-197 Mason granted writer/objectViewer/viewer/logWriter; RBSDK:163 'serviceAccount:«MASON_EMAIL» with roles/artifactregistry.writer' re-asserted in director invest.
findings.suggested_fix: Decide and state Mason/Envoy's audit status explicitly: either (a) give cloud-native system identities their own declared roster of capability-sets so they fall inside the audit domain (the memo defers this ontological question — but the audit can't), or (b) define an explicit, named allow-set of system principals the surplus scan ignores, with the same 'log the bend, tie it to a removal condition' Pale discipline. Note the second-order hazard: option (b) makes 'is this an RBK-managed system SA' a trust input the surplus scan must get right, or an attacker-planted binding on a Mason-named principal escapes the tripwire.

findings.title: Concurrent grant vs audit auto-converge can fight; no roster-write/IAM ordering or locking specified
findings.location: memo §The declared roster (§Physical home / etag-guarded RMW) and §The audit (§auto-converge)
findings.issue: The model has two writers to IAM that can run concurrently: the grant verb (writes roster first, then setIamPolicy) and the audit's deficit auto-converge (reads roster, re-runs idempotent grant). The memo mandates etag-guarded RMW for the roster object and notes IAM's own single-writer etag invariant, but never specifies the ORDERING contract between the roster write and the IAM write, nor what happens when audit auto-converge races a concurrent revoke. Concrete split-brain: grant writes roster (intent +set) but its setIamPolicy loses the etag race and is not retried to completion — now the roster declares a set whose bindings are absent. The audit will read this as deficit and auto-converge (correct, self-healing). But the symmetric case is unhandled: a revoke that removes the roster entry, races a concurrent audit that already read the OLD roster and auto-converges the just-revoked set back into IAM — the audit silently re-grants what an operator just revoked. The memo's asymmetric-healing reasoning ('never auto-revoke') addresses surplus but not this deficit-side resurrection-by-stale-read.
findings.evidence: memo:88-91 roster is 'mutable, high-churn, etag-guarded read-modify-write (like IAM policies themselves)'; memo:111-112 'Deficit is ours ... auto-converge up to D (re-run the idempotent grant)'; memo:114-116 surplus handling stated, but no deficit-side stale-read / concurrent-revoke contract.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a concurrency contract: (1) grant writes roster THEN IAM, and IAM-write failure must roll the roster back or mark the entry pending so a partial grant is not read as authoritative intent; (2) audit auto-converge must re-read the roster under etag immediately before each grant (not from a snapshot), so a concurrent revoke wins; (3) state that revoke writes IAM THEN roster (so a crash mid-revoke leaves the entry declared-but-absent = a healable deficit, not an orphaned grant). This is the deficit-side mirror of the surplus 'never auto-revoke' rule.

findings.title: Partial multi-scope revoke (revoke counterpart of deficit-fragment) is unhandled in the heal taxonomy
findings.location: memo §The audit (drift taxonomy table) vs §Identity verbs ('remove = revoke-all then delete')
findings.issue: The drift taxonomy covers half-failed multi-scope GRANT (deficit-fragment, auto-heal) but never the symmetric half-failed multi-scope REVOKE. A director set spans project + Mason SA + repo + bucket (memo:77, 'several bindings smeared across project + Mason SA + repo + bucket'). If revoke-all fails partway — exactly the failure class memo-20260604's Class-C addendum documents on the resource-scope revoke path (repo getIamPolicy 403 at 0/3/9/21/41/61s) — the citizen is left holding a fragment of a set the roster no longer declares. Under the taxonomy that is surplus-fragment ('bindings belonging to no whole set'), which the model REPORTS rather than heals, and explicitly 'Never auto-revoke.' So a routine half-failed revoke degrades to a human-adjudicated surplus rather than being retried as the operator's own incomplete operation — the same fragment, classified as adversarial-Pale instead of ours. The Pale recognition test ('can I edit the source?') would call an incomplete revoke OURS, but the taxonomy's origin-blindness sends it to the report-and-wait path.
findings.evidence: memo:106-107 surplus-fragment 'Heal: report'; memo:115-116 'Never auto-revoke ... the destructive operation is always human-driven'; memo-20260604 addendum:164-169 resource-scope revoke 403-flap is a documented, transient, ours-side failure that retries clean.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a revoke-fragment state to the taxonomy: a fragment whose set was declared until this operator's in-flight revoke. Track revoke intent (e.g. a roster 'revoking' tombstone) so the audit can recognize an interrupted revoke as ours and finish it idempotently, distinct from an out-of-band surplus that must stay human-adjudicated. Cross-reference memo-20260604's Class-C revoke tolerance as the propagation machinery this reuses.

findings.title: Federation 'just swap identity-kind' seam unverified against the roster-write authority placement
findings.location: memo §Scope ('federation plugs in later by swapping identity-kind and token mint and nothing else') and §The declared roster (§Writers / §Invariant)
findings.issue: The model's headline claim is that federation plugs in by swapping only identity-kind, the principal handle, and token mint. But the memo's own roster invariant requires roster-write authority to be 'at least as protected as the grant verbs (setIamPolicy)' — and in federation that protection lands on a `principal://` IdP-subject ACL, a different ACL substrate than the keyfile tier's SA-key possession. The memo never checks that the GCS-object (or GAR-artifact) roster home can express equivalent write-ACL protection for federate principals, nor who writes a federate's roster entry (memo:94 says 'governor writes director/retriever; payor writes governor' — but a federate has no governor-minted SA; its add 'is first grant', so the writer-authority mapping is unstated for the federate case). The claim 'nothing else differs' is asserted, not surveyed, precisely at the security boundary (roster-write authority) the memo elsewhere calls 'a security decision, not only storage.'
findings.evidence: memo:13-14 'federation plugs in later by swapping identity-kind and token mint and nothing else'; memo:120-122 roster home 'must satisfy that ACL constraint — it is a security boundary'; memo:94 writers mapping is SA/governor-centric with no federate row; memo:65-66 federate 'add ≈ first grant (identity owned by the IdP, not minted here)'.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-paragraph federation-seam check confirming (or flagging open) that the chosen roster home can enforce grant-parity write-ACLs for `principal://` federate entries, and add the federate row to the Writers mapping (who is authorized to write a federate's roster entry, given there is no governor-minted SA and 'add = first grant'). If unverifiable now, list it as an explicit open under the federation claim rather than inside the blanket 'nothing else differs.'

result.reasoning:
I independently verified the cited lines. Lines 174-175 do read verbatim "a standing owner-holding citizen buys nothing and is pure attack surface; drop or demote it" (the finding cites this accurately). The substantive part of the finding — that the memo flags open decisions inline elsewhere but not here — is real: grep confirms an established inline-open convention (line 88 "**Physical home (open).**", line 31 "... is deferred", line 238 "an open scoping question — see the paddock"). The authority section spanning lines 149-184 carries no such "(open)" marker on the drop/demote choice, which is a genuine inconsistency with the memo's own pattern. I could not verify "Paddock line 145" because the paddock text was supplied as "undefined", but the memo independently corroborates the open status (line 238's "see the paddock" deferral and the document-wide paddock-pointer posture), so the underlying claim stands without the paddock.

However the finding overstates and slightly mischaracterizes the issue, so it is partial rather than fully real: (1) The section is not entirely silent — "drop or demote it" is itself an unresolved disjunction, a soft signal. (2) The memo presents "buys nothing / pure attack surface" as a reached conclusion for the keyfile/solo case, not as an unfrozen premise; what is actually open is the implementation choice (drop vs demote vs ephemeral governor, line 182-183) and the heat-scoping, not the premise. (3) The suggested fix's wording is wrong: it says "federate-tier analysis may differ," but the federate case is already stated explicitly in the same paragraph (line 175-176: "a condition-limited governor is the right preventive tool") and it manifestly does differ — so that sentence would be redundant-and-misleading. This is a low-severity clarity nit; the existing disjunction plus line 238 partially cover it.
result.refined_fix: Add an inline open-marker consistent with the memo's existing convention (cf. line 88 "**Physical home (open).**"), but flag the implementation+scoping choice, not the premise, and do not claim federate analysis is absent (it is already in the same paragraph). E.g., append to the Tier-dependent bullet (after line 176): "The drop-vs-demote choice for the keyfile tier — and whether this heat resolves it — is open; see the paddock open items. (The ephemeral-governor alternative below is the third option.)" Alternatively, lift the existing "Ephemeral governor" bullet (lines 182-183) up next to the drop/demote disjunction so all three keyfile options sit together and read as an unresolved set rather than a settled conclusion.

findings.title: Audit domain excludes non-roster principals — escalation via a brand-new SA is invisible
findings.location: memo §The audit (line 134: "Audit domain = roster-identities × set-defined bindings; non-roster principals are out of scope")
findings.issue: The audit is scoped to roster-identities only. An attacker who has compromised any principal with setIamPolicy on a depot resource (e.g. a leaked director key — director holds repo-scoped roles/artifactregistry.repoAdmin, which carries artifactregistry.repositories.setIamPolicy per RBSDK) can grant a powerful role to a *fresh* principal (a new SA they create, or an external account) that is NOT in the roster. Because the audit never enumerates that principal, the surplus-detection mechanism — described as the escalation tripwire when authority rests on a standing governor (memo §Authority, "surplus-detection the only escalation tripwire") — never fires. The whole surplus taxonomy (surplus-complete/surplus-fragment) is computed per-roster-identity, so a grant to a non-roster member is structurally outside what the audit looks at. The design's own claim that surplus is 'a clean someone-bypassed-the-verbs signal' (line 127) holds only for roster members; the most natural escalation (grant to a member I control that you've never heard of) is silent.
findings.evidence: memo line 134 scopes the audit to 'roster-identities × set-defined bindings'; RBSDK-director_invest.adoc line 162 grants director `roles/artifactregistry.repoAdmin` (carries `artifactregistry.repositories.setIamPolicy`); memo lines 166-168 acknowledge this repoAdmin setIamPolicy residual is 'covered by the detective audit' — but the detective audit as defined cannot see a grant to a non-roster principal.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a second, complementary audit axis: enumerate the *actual members* on every depot resource policy and flag any RBK-relevant binding whose member is neither a roster identity nor an expected system identity (Mason, payor, future Envoy). The membership-driven sweep is the dual of the identity-driven sweep; without it 'surplus' only catches grants onto known identities. State explicitly that the audit must walk policies member-first, not only roster-first.

findings.title: Roster-write-≥-grant invariant is necessary but not sufficient; it ignores resource-scope setIamPolicy holders
findings.location: memo §The audit (lines 118-122, 'Invariant the asymmetry rests on') and §Authority (lines 149-168)
findings.issue: The auto-converge-deficit safety argument rests on a single invariant: roster-write authority ≥ grant authority (setIamPolicy). But 'grant authority' in this depot is not a single thing. The memo itself establishes that resource-scoped grants (repo/bucket/SA) are NOT IAM-limitable (lines 169-172) and that the director already holds repo-scope setIamPolicy via repoAdmin. So the real grant-authority floor is whoever can write any RBK-relevant resource policy — which includes every director. If the roster lives in the depot GCS bucket (memo line 88, the stated natural fit), then any principal with storage write on that bucket can write the roster. The invariant therefore must be: roster-write authority ≥ the *strongest* setIamPolicy reachable by any operator identity, including resource-scope. The memo's wording ('at least as protected as the grant verbs') reads as project-scope setIamPolicy and does not engage the resource-scope holders the very same memo enumerates as uncloseable preventively. An attacker holding a director key who can also write the roster bucket can declare themselves a new capability-set, then let auto-converge grant it — turning a detective-only surplus into an auto-applied escalation.
findings.evidence: memo lines 118-122 state the invariant as roster-write ≥ setIamPolicy; memo lines 169-172 establish resource-scoped grants are not IAM-limitable and the audit covers the residual; RBSDK line 162 gives director repo-scope setIamPolicy. The bucket-home option (line 88) places roster writes behind storage IAM, a different and potentially weaker ACL than setIamPolicy.
findings.suggested_fix: Restate the invariant against the strongest reachable setIamPolicy (explicitly including resource-scope repoAdmin), and add a hard constraint: the roster home's write-ACL must NOT be grantable by any operator capability-set (so a director cannot acquire roster-write through any path). Concretely this likely rules out the depot GCS bucket if directors hold any storage-admin there; prefer a home whose write authority is held only by governor/payor and is not itself a member of any capability-set. Make 'roster-write cannot be a capability' an explicit cinch.

findings.title: Auto-converge re-grants a roster entry an attacker can author before the human sees the diff
findings.location: memo §The audit (lines 109-122, asymmetric healing / auto-converge-deficit)
findings.issue: The asymmetry — deficit auto-converges, surplus is reported — creates an attacker-favorable race even when the roster-write ACL is correct, because the trust boundary is 'who can write the roster' and the model never requires that a roster write be *attributable* or *reviewed* before auto-heal acts on it. The design says auto-converge re-runs the idempotent grant 'up to D' (declared). If an attacker who has (by any means) obtained roster-write injects a new (identity, capability-set) row, the next audit sees a deficit (declared but not in IAM) and auto-grants it — logged but not gated. The 'logged, never silent' property is post-hoc; it does not stop the grant. The surplus path was deliberately made human-gated precisely because a write to intent shouldn't auto-apply a privilege change — but a deficit produced by a *roster addition* is exactly that same privilege change wearing the deficit costume. The taxonomy collapses 'our grant half-failed' and 'someone added intent' into one auto-healing bucket.
findings.evidence: memo lines 110-112: 'Deficit is ours ... auto-converge up to D (re-run the idempotent grant), logged, never silent.' lines 124-127 argue tooling 'cannot produce a surplus absent a definition change' but say nothing preventing a malicious roster *addition* from manifesting as an auto-healed deficit. The invariant (lines 118-122) protects who-can-write but not whether a write is reviewed.
findings.suggested_fix: Distinguish two deficit causes by provenance: (a) a binding the tooling itself previously placed and that later vanished (legitimately ours — auto-heal), versus (b) a roster row with no corresponding prior successful grant in our own log (a new intent assertion — should require the same human adjudication as surplus before first grant). Tie auto-converge to 'we have a record of having granted this before,' not merely 'declared ∧ absent.' Equivalently: a roster *addition* enters as pending and is human-confirmed on first apply; only re-convergence of an already-applied row is automatic.

findings.title: Surplus-via-definition-shrink gives malicious revocation cover, but also can mask malicious *expansion*
findings.location: memo §The audit (lines 124-130, definition-shrink surplus + capability-set definition change)
findings.issue: The memo notes the one tooling-side surplus source is a capability-set definition *shrink*, and uses this to justify report-not-revoke for all surplus (line 130). But capability-set definitions are code (line 85), and the audit compares actual IAM to the *current* code definition. An attacker who can land a code change that *expands* a capability-set definition (adds a powerful binding) converts every roster member of that set into an auto-converge deficit on the next audit — the audit will silently grant the new binding to all holders, framed as routine deficit-healing. The memo treats definition change as benign 'deficit-on-additions, auto-heal' (line 131) with 'no special versioning machinery.' That is precisely the danger: a definition expansion is a privilege grant to every holder, applied automatically, with the same logged-but-ungated treatment as a failed grant retry. The trust boundary silently shifts from 'who can setIamPolicy' to 'who can merge a one-line change to the capability-set definitions.'
findings.evidence: memo line 85 'Capability-set definitions are code'; line 131 'A capability-set definition change decomposes into deficit-on-additions (auto-heal ...) ... no special versioning machinery'; lines 105-107 audit 'auto-converge' deficit. The asymmetry treats all additions as ours-to-heal regardless of whether the addition is a code-review event with security weight.
findings.suggested_fix: Treat a capability-set *definition expansion* as a privilege change requiring the same gate as a surplus legitimization, not as routine deficit auto-heal. At minimum, require that a definition change which increases bindings be human-acknowledged on first roster-wide apply (a 'definition bump' confirmation), distinct from re-converging a transient grant failure. Note in the memo that 'who can edit the capability-set definitions' is a first-class authority equal to grant authority.

findings.title: Orphan description-sentinel marker is forgeable and strippable by the SA's own operator
findings.location: memo §Orphan hazard (lines 136-147) and §Verified facts (lines 216-220)
findings.issue: The fallback orphan marker is an SA `description` sentinel ('rbk:…'). serviceAccounts.update (which writes description/displayName) is a standard SA-management permission; any principal with `iam.serviceAccounts.update` on the depot project can write or clear it. The governor (owner) obviously can, but so can anyone the governor's capability-set or a compromised governor grants SA-admin to. Two concrete failures: (1) an attacker can *strip* the sentinel from a real RBK-managed SA so the orphan-sweep no longer recognizes it as managed (defeating the sweep's purpose — finding stranded live keys), and (2) an attacker can *forge* 'rbk:' on an unrelated SA to make the sweep treat a foreign SA as managed and potentially act on it. The memo presents the sentinel as a 'stable fallback' earning 'exactly this one job' but does not engage that the marker is mutable by the same authority surface that mints SAs. SA tags (the preferred option) have a separate, project/org-scoped tag-binding ACL and are stronger here — the description sentinel is the weak option precisely because it sits in operator-writable SA metadata.
findings.evidence: memo lines 143-144 'SA description sentinel (rbk:…): stable fallback'; GCP serviceAccounts.update writes description and is grantable via roles touching `iam.serviceAccounts.update`. The memo's own §Authority warns the governor must not hold plain owner (line 163), implying SA-management authority is broadly distributable — the same authority that can rewrite a description.
findings.suggested_fix: State that the orphan sweep is integrity-advisory, not authoritative: it can find probable orphans but a stripped/forged sentinel must not be trusted. Prefer SA tags (tag-binding ACL is separable from SA-update) and, regardless of marker, cross-check the orphan sweep against the roster + key inventory rather than the marker alone — a live USER_MANAGED key on an SA absent from the roster is an orphan whether or not it carries the sentinel. Don't let marker absence/presence be the sole signal.

findings.title: Divest decomposition omits the roster write — leaving a 'declared but revoked' deficit that auto-converge will resurrect
findings.location: memo §Verb dissolution (line 49: 'divest | revoke(all held) + remove-citizen') vs §The audit auto-converge
findings.issue: Verb dissolution decomposes divest into 'revoke(all held) + remove-citizen' (line 49) but does not state that remove-citizen *also deletes the roster entry*, and the ordering is unspecified. If the roster row survives a divest (or is deleted after a revoke but a residual capability-set row remains), the audit sees declared-but-absent = deficit = auto-converge, and re-grants capabilities to a citizen you just tore down. This is the same monotonic-accumulation class as the H1 binding-leak (memo-20260604) but worse: it actively *re-provisions* a revoked identity. The asymmetric-healing design assumes the roster is always the authoritative present intent, so any path that revokes IAM without synchronously zeroing the roster row turns auto-heal into auto-resurrection. The current divest spec (RBSDD) is fail-loud and does not touch any roster (rosters today are derived/emitted read-only per RBSDR), so the new model introduces a write-ordering obligation that the verb decomposition does not pin down.
findings.evidence: memo line 49 'divest | revoke(all held) + remove-citizen'; memo lines 105-112 auto-converge any declared-but-absent deficit; RBSDD-director_divest.adoc revokes bindings and deletes the SA but never writes a roster (RBSDR is read-only emission). No step sequences roster-delete before/with revoke.
findings.suggested_fix: Specify divest as: delete the roster row(s) FIRST (intent withdrawn), THEN revoke IAM, THEN remove the citizen — so no window exists where declared>actual makes auto-converge re-grant a torn-down identity. Add an explicit invariant: 'no IAM revoke without a prior or atomic roster withdrawal,' the dual of 'grant writes intent first.' Call out the auto-resurrection failure mode as the reason ordering is load-bearing.

findings.title: Union key on one citizen + tier-blind claim understates federate compromise blast radius vs the memo's own bounding
findings.location: memo §Blast radius (lines 186-200)
findings.issue: The blast-radius section bounds the union-key risk as 'resting-key form is citizen-tier-only' and reassures that 'most citizens wear one hat.' But the multi-role union is the explicit feature (one citizen = one SA = union of capabilities, line 188-191), and the section frames the federate case as merely 'a hijacked live session rather than a file at rest' (line 197) — quietly weaker framing for the tier the whole heat exists to enable. A federate granted governor+director+retriever, hijacked mid-session, wields owner-equivalent authority with central revocation as the only backstop; the memo presents this as comparable-but-bounded without noting that the federation session cap (memo-20260527: 15min–12h, default 1h) is the only thing shrinking that window, and that a persisted refresh token (memo-20260527 test-rig residual) reintroduces a resting secret that re-creates the citizen-tier resting-key risk it claims federation removes. The 'tier-blind union property' is true, but the security *cost asymmetry* (resting file vs session+session-cap+possible refresh-token) is not surfaced where the reader weighs accepting the union.
findings.evidence: memo lines 196-198 frame federate exposure as 'hijacked live session rather than a file at rest'; memo-20260527 lines 117-121 (session cap 15min–12h) and 137-152 (refresh-token confined to test rig) show the bounding mechanism and the one residual that breaks it. The citizen memo's blast-radius section does not cite the session cap or the refresh-token residual as part of the federate union risk.
findings.suggested_fix: In §Blast radius, name the federation session cap as the mechanism bounding the federate union window, and flag the test-rig persisted refresh token as the one path that reintroduces a resting union secret — so 'union is tier-blind but the resting-secret cost is citizen-only' is stated with its single exception, not as an unqualified reassurance.

findings.title: Governor topology per tier: ephemeral/keyfile-delegated governor is offered without closing the key-at-rest escalation it creates
findings.location: memo §Authority (lines 174-183, tier-dependent topology + ephemeral governor)
findings.issue: The memo recommends, for keyfile/solo, dropping or demoting a standing owner-holding citizen governor ('pure attack surface', line 175), and offers an 'ephemeral governor: grant the governor-set for one op, then revoke' (lines 182-183) as the decoupling-enabled option. But in the keyfile tier the governor *is* an SA with a key file on disk (RBSGM generates a governor RBRA). An ephemeral governor that is granted the governor-set, used, then revoked still mints and leaves a key file unless that file is also destroyed synchronously — and the grant/revoke window is exactly when the key is most powerful. The model decouples capability from identity, so 'revoke the governor-set' leaves the governor *identity and its key* intact with capabilities stripped; if the revoke half races or fails (the model's own Class-C / propagation reality, memo-20260604 addendum), the key sits on disk holding owner. The ephemeral pattern reduces *resting* exposure only if key destruction is coupled to capability revoke, which the memo does not require. Worse, 'grant governor-set for one op' is itself a roster/grant write that, per the auto-converge invariant, must be at least as protected as setIamPolicy — circular when the thing being granted IS setIamPolicy-bearing owner.
findings.evidence: memo lines 182-183 'grant the governor-set for one op, then revoke'; memo lines 61-67 identity verbs (rekey rotates key, capability revoke leaves key) — so capability-revoke alone leaves the key; RBSGM-governor_mantle.adoc generates a governor RBRA (key on disk); memo-20260604 addendum documents revoke-path Class-C 403 races (revoke can lag), so the disabling step is not instantaneous.
findings.suggested_fix: If the ephemeral governor pattern is retained, require that capability-revoke be coupled with key destruction (rekey-to-nothing or SA delete) for the keyfile tier, since stripping capabilities does not neutralize a resting owner-key. Note that during the grant→use→revoke window the on-disk key carries full owner and any revoke lag extends that window. Prefer the stated default (no standing keyfile governor; payor as the grant authority) and mark the ephemeral keyfile governor as not-recommended rather than a neutral option.

findings.title: Vocabulary defined twice — paddock re-defines terms the memo owns
findings.location: paddock §The citizen model (gazette lines 27-47) vs memo §Scope and vocabulary (lines 12-31)
findings.issue: The citizen / federate / capability-set / declared-roster / holdings definitions are spelled out in full in BOTH documents. Paddock line 27 ('Citizen = a keyfile-tier operator identity ... a depot-issued SA holding one RBRA key') and memo line 17-18 ('Citizen — a keyfile-tier operator identity: a depot-issued service account holding one RBRA key') are the same definition. Same for capability-set (paddock 36-37 / memo 24) and the ontology bullet (paddock 46-47 / memo 28-31). A paddock holds shape; term *definitions* are reference material the memo already owns. Defining them twice means a vocabulary edit must land in two places or they drift.
findings.evidence: paddock: 'Citizen = a keyfile-tier operator identity (governor/director/retriever), a depot-issued SA holding one RBRA key'; memo line 17: 'Citizen — a keyfile-tier operator identity: a depot-issued service account holding one RBRA key'
findings.suggested_fix: In the paddock, name the four terms in one line and point to the memo's vocabulary section for definitions (the paddock already does this for the audit taxonomy: '(Drift taxonomy ... : memo.)'). Keep only the rank-neutral / multi-role-is-first-class CLAIMS (shape decisions); drop the definitional clauses.

findings.title: "What done looks like" enumerates concrete surfaces — a paddock site-enumeration
findings.location: paddock §What done looks like (gazette lines 104-114)
findings.issue: The section lists six concrete migration surfaces (workstation bash, governor/payor admin verbs, cloud-side bash, .adoc specs, Rust theurge cases, onboarding handbook tracks) plus parenthetical mechanism for two of them. JJK paddock/docket posture explicitly names 'Site enumerations' as an anti-pattern and 'Mechanism leaking into the paddock' as a posture violation. The parentheticals '(role-keyed selection collapses to one identity accessor)' and 'generalizing memo-20260527's "single code seam"' are mechanism already held in the memo's §memo-20260527 divergence (memo lines 234-238).
findings.evidence: paddock: 'route the role/file-path-keyed token-accessor calls through one identity-keyed accessor (generalizing memo-20260527's "single code seam")' — plus the six-item surface list 'workstation bash ..., the governor/payor admin verbs ..., cloud-side bash ..., the .adoc specs ..., the Rust theurge cases ..., and the onboarding handbook tracks'
findings.suggested_fix: Reduce to shape: 'every cult-verb surface migrates to citizen/capability vocabulary; the load-bearing federation-enabling core is the one identity-keyed token accessor; the broader vocabulary sweep is motivated-but-not-required.' Drop the six-surface roster (a discovery recipe like 'grep role names across Tools/rbk/' is docket-time work, not paddock shape) and move the accessor mechanism parenthetical to the memo.

findings.title: Cinched-decisions bullets near-totally restate the prose sections above them
findings.location: paddock §Cinched decisions (gazette lines 116-135)
findings.issue: Eight of the nine cinched bullets are compressed restatements of prose stated one-to-two screens up: 'Enforcement is server-side IAM' (= §Intent vs enforcement line 51), 'identity is the citizen ... decoupled ... multi-role first-class' (= §The citizen model), 'one SA per person ... tradeoff accepted' (= §Blast radius line 89-90), 'grant/revoke operate only on named sets' (= §The citizen model line 44), 'audit healing is asymmetric' (= §The audit), 'governor breadth resolved per tier' (= §Authority topology line 81), 'cloud-native identities share the abstraction not the lifecycle' (= ontology bullet line 46-47). The doc says nearly everything twice.
findings.evidence: Cinch 'Capability-sets are named code; grant/revoke operate only on named sets, never raw bindings' restates §The citizen model 'Grant/revoke operate only on named capability-sets, never raw bindings'; cinch 'Audit healing is asymmetric: auto-converge deficits, report surplus' restates §The audit in substance.
findings.suggested_fix: Either fold the prose-section conclusions INTO the cinch bullets (drop the standalone prose where the cinch already carries the decision), or trim each cinch to the part not already in prose. Given JJK posture that cinched decisions are the canonical do-not-re-litigate list, prefer making the cinch list authoritative and letting the prose sections carry only the reasoning the cinch omits — not restate the decision.
