findings.title: Auto-heal roster-write invariant stated three-plus times in the paddock
findings.location: paddock §The audit (gazette 66-67), §Open (148-149), cinch line 127
findings.issue: The single invariant 'roster-write authority must be at least as protected as the grant verbs' is repeated across the paddock: in the audit prose ('Auto-converge presumes roster-write authority is at least as protected as the grant verbs ... see Open'), in the Open item ('roster-write authority must be at least as protected as the grant verbs (the auto-heal invariant)'), and again in the cinch ('with auto-converge presuming roster-write is at least as protected as grant'). One statement plus a forward-pointer suffices.
findings.evidence: audit: 'Auto-converge presumes roster-write authority is at least as protected as the grant verbs (else it is a softer escalation path) ... see Open.'; Open: 'roster-write authority must be at least as protected as the grant verbs (the auto-heal invariant).'
findings.suggested_fix: State the invariant once where it has policy force (the Open item on roster physical home), replace the audit-prose restatement with the bare pointer it already half-has ('— a security constraint on where the roster lives; see Open'), and drop the duplicate clause from the cinch bullet.

findings.title: Mechanism leak: GCP-primitive justification in the paddock spine bullet
findings.location: paddock §The citizen model, decoupling bullet (gazette lines 32-35)
findings.issue: 'this is native to the GCP primitive (an SA may exist with zero bindings)' is an implementation justification for why decoupling is achievable — mechanism, not shape. The shape claim is 'identity lifecycle is decoupled from capability lifecycle; today's invest fuses the two, the model unfuses them.' Why GCP permits it belongs in the memo.
findings.evidence: paddock: 'Adding/removing a citizen is independent of granting/revoking its capabilities; this is native to the GCP primitive (an SA may exist with zero bindings).'
findings.suggested_fix: Drop the parenthetical; the decoupling claim stands as shape on its own. The SA-zero-bindings fact, if needed, is implied by the memo's §Identity verbs branch section.

result.reasoning:
I independently verified every cited fact. Memo line 19 does define "Federate" as a noun for the federation-tier OPERATOR identity. RBSHR lines 213 ("it federates to AWS via OIDC Workload Identity Federation") and 238 ("federates to the embassy's IAM role") do use the verb for the envoy (Workload/machine egress). memo-20260527 line 71 confirms "they coexist but never chain for a single actor," and its §"Workforce vs Workload — two federation products" (line 60) maps Workforce→operator-federation, Workload→envoy. So the raw collision the finding describes is real: the federation word-stem is now load-bearing on both sides of the Workforce/Workload seam.

However, the finding overstates the conflation hazard, which is why this is partial, not real:
1. The two usages are different parts of speech — noun "federate" vs verb "federates" — not the identical token the "one grep apart" framing implies.
2. A grep of RBSHR for "federat" shows the envoy is NEVER called a "federate" (noun); it is consistently "envoy." There is no noun-on-noun collision.
3. The disambiguation the finding requests substantially ALREADY EXISTS in the cited corpus: RBSHR line 316 explicitly says the operator-federation tier is "Distinct from the envoy's egress federation (GCP-to-AWS delivery, the machine-identity Workload product)," and memo-20260527 devotes a whole section to Workforce-vs-Workload. The seam is already drawn at the source-document level; the memo just doesn't restate it.
4. "Federate"/"federated principal" is memo-20260527's own near-canonical phrasing (line 86: "a federated principal"), so the noun choice is well-grounded.

The new memo (the artifact under review) is where the disambiguation is genuinely absent, so adding a one-liner there is a legitimate, low-cost improvement not yet present in the current text. But the finding's fix wording ("it is unrelated to the envoy's Workload 'federates to AWS'") is imprecise — it should point at the already-existing RBSHR line 316 / memo-20260527 §Workforce-vs-Workload disambiguation rather than re-deriving it, so the memo stays a pointer (per the project's "point, don't paraphrase" discipline) rather than duplicating the seam description.
result.refined_fix: In the memo's §"Scope and vocabulary", append to the Federate bullet a one-clause pointer rather than a fresh restatement: "(Workforce-federated operator subject — the human-identity product; not the envoy's Workload 'federates to AWS' egress, which RBSHR §Envoy and memo-20260527 §Workforce-vs-Workload already separate.)" This pins the noun to the operator/Workforce side and, per the project's point-don't-paraphrase docket discipline, references the existing disambiguation (RBSHR line 316, memo-20260527 line 60) instead of duplicating it. Do not assert the two are "unrelated" without naming where the seam is already drawn.

result.reasoning:
I independently read both cited files. The collision is real and verbatim-confirmed:

1. Memo line 16-18 defines "Citizen" as a keyfile-tier operator IDENTITY ("a depot-issued service account holding one RBRA key... the home of the identity"). Memo line 27 defines "Holdings" as "the depot's artifacts (arks/hoards/bullions). Property, not identities," and line 28 makes the inversion explicit by building a "civic ontology — citizens/federates (people) + holdings (property)."

2. RBSHR line 96-99 (hoard entry): "A {rbtgi_depot} citizen alongside the {rbtga_ark}" — "citizen" denotes a depot-resident ARTIFACT (a hoard). RBSHR line 222 (bullion entry): "bullions are the new AWS-side citizen with S3-native retention semantics" — "citizen" denotes a delivered ARTIFACT (a bullion). Both are property, the opposite category from the memo's person-identity meaning.

So the memo simultaneously (a) reuses "citizen" for the inverted category and (b) mints "holdings" for exactly what RBSHR's "citizen" currently means. grep confirms the memo never acknowledges this collision (no supersession note for RBSHR's "citizen"); the only divergence notes it carries are for memo-20260527. So this is a genuine, un-flagged conflict against a committed spec — verdict real.

Two qualifications that make the candidate's framing slightly overstated (hence I sharpen rather than fully endorse the fix):

- RBSHR's two "citizen" uses are NOT formal MCM quoins. They are casual/metaphorical prose ("a resident member of the polity"), with no `{citizen}` attribute reference and no `[[citizen]]` anchor — unlike the bracketed linked terms `{rbtgi_depot}`, `{rbtga_ark}` right beside them. The candidate's invocation of the "Quoin Sub-Letter Discipline" (which governs MCM `prefixXY_` sub-prefixes) and "within-domain monosemy" is therefore an imprecise citation — the governing principle is the general minting rule ("any persistent name anywhere is in the mint universe" + semantic uniqueness), not the quoin-internal sub-letter rule. The collision still matters because the memo elevates the metaphor into a formal ontology, but it is a prose/term collision, not a formal-quoin clash.

- RBSHR line 193 explicitly flags the embassy/envoy/BULLION vocabulary as "tentative working language from a design conversation; concepts are firmer than the words." So the bullion "citizen" (line 222) is already-disclaimed-tentative AWS-side language and is the cheap one to change. The hoard "citizen" (line 97) is GCP-side and not flagged tentative — that is the load-bearing collision. This nuance refines the fix.

The candidate's core claim (real, high-stakes term inversion against committed text, un-noted) holds. The proposed fix (rename RBSHR's uses to "holdings" OR pick a different identity noun; add a supersession note) is directionally correct but needs the quoin/prose distinction and the tentative-vocabulary nuance folded in. Hence verdict real, severity adjusted to medium (prose metaphor, not a formal quoin clash; one of the two sites is already self-disclaimed tentative; both are in an unscoped roadmap, not in shipping code/specs that import the term), with a refined fix.
result.refined_fix:
The collision is real and must be resolved before the heat freezes "citizen" as the operator-identity noun, because RBSHR (committed) uses "citizen" for the opposite category (property). But sharpen on two points the candidate missed:

(1) RBSHR's two "citizen" uses are casual prose metaphors ("a resident of the depot/embassy"), NOT formal MCM quoins (no `{citizen}` attribute ref, no `[[citizen]]` anchor — contrast the bracketed `{rbtgi_depot}`/`{rbtga_ark}` beside them). So cite the general minting rule ("any persistent name anywhere is in scope," semantic uniqueness) rather than the Quoin Sub-Letter Discipline / within-domain-monosemy rule, which governs MCM `prefixXY_` sub-prefixes specifically and does not apply here.

(2) RBSHR line 193 already disclaims the embassy/envoy/bullion vocabulary as "tentative working language... concepts are firmer than the words" — so the bullion "citizen" (line 222) is cheap to retire, while the hoard "citizen" (line 97, GCP-side, not flagged tentative) is the load-bearing one.

Resolution, preferred: keep "citizen" for the operator identity and reword RBSHR's two prose uses away from "citizen" — e.g. hoard line 97 "A {rbtgi_depot}-resident artifact alongside the {rbtga_ark}", bullion line 222 "bullions are the new AWS-side artifact/deliverable with S3-native retention semantics" (avoid reusing "holdings" verbatim in RBSHR if that term isn't yet defined there). The memo should add an explicit "supersedes RBSHR's prose 'citizen' (artifact) usage" note, paralleling its existing memo-20260527 divergence note, since RBSHR is committed. Alternatively, pick a different identity noun and leave RBSHR untouched — but the memo still owes a one-line note that "citizen" was checked against RBSHR's prior prose use and deliberately diverged. Do not leave "citizen" meaning property in one spec and person in another with no cross-reference.

result.reasoning:
I read the cited memo (memo-20260605-citizen-capability-model.md) and confirmed the quoted text at lines 237-238 is verbatim accurate: the memo does say "Whether the heat's 'load-bearing core' is the role-keyed no-behavior stepping-stone or the identity-keyed end state is an open scoping question — see the paddock." I also confirmed the supporting evidence: line 187 ("One citizen per person = one SA"), lines 230-231 (the "supersedes both" claim), and the code at rbgg_Governor.sh:658 (`local z_account_name="${RBCC_account_director}-${z_identity}"`), which is indeed role-keyed (role prefix + identity suffix). However, the finding's substance is precisely the carved-out finding the task instructions forbid. The finding's title is "Scoping ambiguity... is unresolved" and its suggested fix is "Clarify in the paddock whether this heat's MVP is (a) role-keyed... or (b) identity-keyed" — i.e., decide the scope. The finding's secondary claim — that lines 230-231's "supersedes both" must be softened to "incremental" under scope (a) — also fails. The finding also leans on "the paddock is undefined (per user statement)" to argue the question is unresolvable, but the paddock arriving as `undefined` to this review is a harness artifact, not evidence about the committed paddock's actual content. The real paddock may legitimately hold this as a documented open.

Net: real quote, real code, but the requested action is exactly the forbidden "decide the scope" finding dressed as a clarity issue, and the memo already handles the open honestly. No genuine, fileable defect.

findings.title: 10-key-per-SA quota unaddressed against citizen rekey lifecycle
findings.location: memo §Identity verbs branch / §Verb dissolution (rekey)
findings.issue: The model introduces a dedicated `rekey` verb ('rotate the key, bindings untouched') as a first-class citizen-tier operation, and the whole pitch is that capability lifecycle is decoupled from key lifecycle so you can rekey freely. But GCP enforces a hard limit of 10 user-managed keys per service account. A naive rekey that mints-new-then-deletes-old, or any rekey that fails partway (create succeeds, delete fails — exactly the orphan-hazard class the memo worries about elsewhere), accumulates keys toward that ceiling; the 11th rekey errors. The memo never states the create-vs-delete ordering for rekey nor acknowledges the quota as a constraint the verb must respect.
findings.evidence: Memo line 65: 'rekey = rotate the key, bindings untouched'. Google IAM quotas: 'Service account keys for a service account: 10' (https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/quotas). The memo's own orphan-hazard section (lines 136-139) shows the team already reasons about half-failed key operations.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-line constraint to the rekey description: rekey must delete-before-create (or sweep stale keys) and is bounded by the 10-key-per-SA quota; a leaked-but-undeleted key consumes quota. Tie this to the orphan-sweep so a stranded key is both findable and quota-reclaimable.

findings.title: Conditioned-governor design omits the 12-logic-operator-per-condition CEL limit
findings.location: memo §Authority — 'IAM can scope grants by role'
findings.issue: The memo accurately captures the modifiedGrantsByRole constraints it cites (project/folder/org only, ≤10 roles, no &&/|| joining of hasOnly, service-recognition caveat, no setIamPolicy-bearing roles). But the conditioned governor it prescribes ('give it a conditioned setIamPolicy instead' of plain roles/owner) lives inside an IAM Condition expression, and GCP caps a role binding's condition expression at 12 logic operators. A condition that combines a modifiedGrantsByRole hasOnly with the resource/date guards a real owner-replacement condition tends to accrue can hit that ceiling. The memo treats the condition as a single hasOnly clause but a production governor condition is rarely that clean.
findings.evidence: Memo lines 156-163 enumerate four modifiedGrantsByRole limits but not the operator cap. Google IAM quotas: 'Logic operators in a role binding's condition expression: 12' (https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/quotas).
findings.suggested_fix: Note in the limits list that the governor's conditioned-setIamPolicy binding shares the 12-logic-operator ceiling with any other guards in the same condition, so the condition must stay simple (the no-&&/|| rule already pushes toward one hasOnly per binding — make that consequence explicit).

findings.title: SA-tags 'condition-usable' overstates what the Preview supports for grant-scoping

findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to blast-radius or the isomorphism paragraph noting that citizen-tier identities consume a finite per-project SA quota whereas federates do not — a real (if distant) tier asymmetry beyond the resting-key difference already cited.

result.reasoning: I independently read the memo and all five cited specs. Every citation is accurate: RBSDK:30 and RBSRK:33 compose `${RBCC_account_director|retriever}-<identity>`; RBSDR:37 filters/strips `^${RBCC_account_director}-(.+)@${RBGD_SA_EMAIL_FULL}$`; RBSDD:28 synthesizes `${RBCC_account_director}-<identity>@...`; RBSRR:43,51,59 define three role-keyed RBRA paths (retriever/director/governor `rbra.env`). The memo lines (187, 72-73, 75-76) are quoted verbatim. The finding's central logic is sound and load-bearing: "one citizen per person = one SA = one key carrying the union" (memo:187) is achievable ONLY if the SA is named identity-only; with `director-alice` + `retriever-alice` you get two SAs = two keys = the old per-role model the memo explicitly discards (memo:189-190). The role-prefix machinery in all four verbs plus RBSRR's three role-keyed paths cannot survive identity-only naming. This is a genuine completeness gap. However, the finding overstates the memo's blindness in two ways. (1) The memo is NOT wholly silent on naming migration: lines 236-238 flag the per-person migration as an open scoping question ("the identity-keyed accessor ... presupposes the per-person migration ... not itself no-behavior-change"), and lines 208-210 explicitly handle the analogous governor naming migration ("needs a stable governor name in place of today's datestamped governor-YYYYMMDDHHmm, plus a one-time migration of existing datestamped governors"). So "treats the departure as already-done rather than as work" and "none appear in 'what done looks like'" are too strong — the memo flags it as open work and solved the parallel governor case. (2) Per the task framing, scoping/heat-chopping is intentionally deferred, and "does this heat perform the per-person migration" is itself partly the open scoping question already named. What survives and is genuinely, valuably missing is narrower than the finding states: (a) the memo never ENUMERATES the concrete director/retriever spec surfaces the migration touches (RBSDK/RBSRK composition, RBSDR filter, RBSDD synthesis, RBSRR's three paths), even though it enumerated the governor's; and (b) it never states the existing-SA cutover mechanics — GCP cannot rename an SA, so director-*/retriever-* SAs require delete+recreate, re-entering the divest revoke-before-delete + gone-confirm-streak race (RBSDD) and the invest create-propagation race (RBSDK), exactly the memo-20260604 flap. The suggested fix's GCP claim (rename impossible; delete-old + new-identity-SA re-enters the DRS race) is technically correct per the specs. Verdict partial: real and high-value gap, but the finding misstates the memo as treating the migration as already-done when it actually flags it open and handles the governor analog; the fix should be scoped to enumeration + existing-SA cutover, not framed as wholly absent.
result.refined_fix: Add a short "Citizen naming migration" subsection to the memo that does for director/retriever what lines 208-210 already do for the governor. It should: (1) state explicitly that the citizen SA name becomes identity-only (`alice`, not `director-alice`), since this is the precondition the one-SA-per-person / union-key claim (memo:187-190) and the lossy-IAM-roster justification (memo:73-76) both rest on; (2) enumerate the concrete director/retriever surfaces that change — RBSDK/RBSRK account-name composition drops the role prefix, RBSDD email synthesis becomes identity-only, RBSDR's prefix-filter roster is retired in favor of the declared-roster read (consistent with the memo's own "roster from IAM is lossy" point), and RBSRR's three role-keyed `{role}/rbra.env` paths collapse toward one identity-keyed path; (3) state the existing-SA cutover: GCP cannot rename an SA, so any deployed `director-*`/`retriever-*` SA migrates by delete-old + invest-new-identity-SA, which re-enters the divest revoke-before-delete + gone-confirm-streak race (RBSDD) and the invest create/key-propagation race (RBSDK) — i.e. the memo-20260604 churn/flap, so the migration must run through the rbk-08 revoke layer + Class-C propagation tolerance, mirroring the governor leak fold-in. Frame this as completing the migration enumeration the memo already started for the governor, not as work the memo never recognized — and leave whether-this-heat-does-it under the existing open scoping flag (memo:238), since that is intentionally deferred.

result.reasoning:
I read the memo (Memos/memo-20260605-citizen-capability-model.md), RBSDD-director_divest.adoc, and memo-20260604 to check the finding's claims independently.

CORE ISSUE IS REAL. The memo develops a strong SURPLUS-side asymmetry ("never auto-revoke ... a stale roster would deprovision a working identity," lines 114-116) but never states its DEFICIT-side mirror. The deficit path auto-acts: "Deficit is ours → auto-converge up to D (re-run the idempotent grant)" (lines 111-112), where D is computed from the declared roster (line 98). The memo nowhere specifies (a) that the audit must read the roster fresh per-converge rather than from a snapshot, nor (b) the ordering of roster-write vs IAM-write on REVOKE. The grant ordering IS stated ("grant writes intent first," line 126) — so the finding's blanket claim "never specifies the ORDERING contract" is partly inaccurate for grant — but the revoke ordering and the audit's read-freshness are genuinely unspecified. With those unspecified, the concrete race holds: revoke removes the roster entry and the IAM bindings; a concurrent audit that already read the OLD roster still counts that set in D, sees A missing it, and auto-converges — silently re-granting what was just revoked. This is exactly the deficit-side resurrection the memo's surplus reasoning never covers. Concurrency is a live concern in this codebase (memo-20260604's Class-C race exists because mantle runs immediately before divest; CLAUDE.md notes multiple officia run concurrently; the roster itself is "high-churn, etag-guarded RMW like IAM policies," line 88), and the audit's auto-converge is described as automatic/unattended ("logged, never silent"), so the race is plausible. Severity medium is right — it is a silent re-grant (security-relevant) but requires a concurrency window.

FIX IS PARTLY WRONG. The finding's suggested fix part (3) — "revoke writes IAM THEN roster (so a crash mid-revoke leaves declared-but-absent = a healable deficit)" — is backwards and re-introduces the very bug. A declared-but-absent state IS a deficit, which the memo AUTO-CONVERGES (re-grants) — i.e., the operator's revoke gets undone. The memo's own grant logic dictates the correct mirror: grant writes roster FIRST precisely so a crashed grant lands as an (auto-healable) deficit, never a surplus (line 126). By symmetry, REVOKE must write roster FIRST (withdraw intent), THEN revoke IAM — so a crashed revoke lands as a SURPLUS of bindings-for-a-no-longer-declared-set, which the memo handles by report-only/never-auto-revoke (the safe, human-driven side). You want a crashed revoke to land on the report side, not the auto-act side. Finding parts (1) "roll back/mark pending" is also over-engineered: a grant whose IAM write fails after the roster write is already a healable deficit under the memo's design — no rollback needed. Only part (2) (re-read roster under etag immediately before each converge) survives intact.
result.refined_fix:
Add a deficit-side concurrency contract that mirrors the existing surplus "never auto-revoke" rule:
(1) REVOKE ordering — withdraw intent FIRST: remove the roster entry, THEN revoke the IAM bindings. This is the exact mirror of the memo's stated grant rule (line 126, "grant writes intent first"). A crash mid-revoke then leaves roster-absent + IAM-present = a SURPLUS, which the memo already routes to report-only/human-adjudicate (safe). The finding's proposed IAM-then-roster ordering is backwards — it would leave a crashed revoke as a declared-but-absent DEFICIT that auto-converge silently re-grants, undoing the operator's revoke.
(2) AUDIT read-freshness — auto-converge must re-read the declared roster under its etag immediately before each grant it issues (not from a snapshot taken at audit start), so a concurrent revoke that has withdrawn intent wins the race and the set is not resurrected.
(3) State explicitly in the memo that the deficit/auto-converge side carries the same staleness hazard the surplus side does, resolved by (1)+(2), making the asymmetry symmetric in its concurrency handling.
Drop the finding's part-1 rollback/mark-pending: a grant whose IAM write fails after the roster write is already a self-healing deficit under the memo's design.

result.reasoning:
I read the cited memo (memo-20260605-citizen-capability-model.md) and the enabler (memo-20260604, including its 2026-06-05 addendum) end-to-end. The finding's factual claims check out:

1. The drift taxonomy (memo:101-107) DOES have a deficit-fragment row ("half-failed multi-scope grant" → auto-converge) with NO symmetric revoke-fragment row. The surplus-fragment row's likely-cause column lists only "tampering / shrunk definition" — it omits an interrupted multi-scope revoke.

2. The "several bindings smeared across project + Mason SA + repo + bucket" quote (memo:77) is accurate, confirming a director set is genuinely multi-scope and revoke-all can partially fail.

3. The memo-20260604 addendum evidence is accurately quoted (lines 164-169): the resource-scope revoke path is a documented, transient, ours-side failure — repo getIamPolicy returned 403 at 0/3/9/21/41/61s and 200 at 81s. So a multi-scope revoke can half-fail mid-operation in exactly the way the finding describes.

4. revoke is NOT only the human-adjudicated surplus path: divest = revoke(all held) + remove-citizen (memo:50) and remove = revoke-all then delete (memo:65) are routine tooling verbs that auto-revoke. Yet memo:116 asserts "the destructive operation is always human-driven" and "Never auto-revoke." This is an internal tension the half-failed-revoke case lands squarely in.

The finding's mechanism is sound: under the natural roster-first ordering (mirroring the explicit grant ordering, memo:126 "grant writes intent *first*"), a half-failed divest removes the declaration then partially revokes IAM, leaving a fragment the roster no longer declares = surplus-fragment = report/never-auto-revoke. So an operator's own incomplete, already-authorized divest gets misclassified as adversarial-Pale tampering and bounced to human adjudication — origin-blind, exactly as the finding states. The Pale test ("can I edit the source?") would correctly call an interrupted-but-authorized revoke OURS.

Strongest counter-argument I checked: memo:126-130 already treats ONE tooling-side surplus source (capability-set definition shrink) and deliberately routes it to report-not-revoke "regardless of origin." This shows the model's stance is intentionally origin-blind for surplus. But the definition-shrink case has no in-flight authorized operation — it's a static config change where human confirmation before deletion is reasonable. The half-failed-revoke case is categorically different: an operator-authorized destructive op that simply didn't complete due to a transient 403, which the memo-20260604 fix already retries clean within the operation. So the gap is real and not subsumed.

This is a genuine completeness gap, not a fabrication. Marking partial because the suggested fix overweights the audit/tombstone mechanism: the primary repair is that the divest/remove verb retries its own revoke idempotently in-operation (it already carries the Class-C tolerance from memo-20260604 1a66443e); the audit-level revoke-intent marker is only needed for the rarer fully-abandoned-mid-divest case. The fix should name both tiers.
result.refined_fix:
Address the gap in two tiers rather than only the audit-tombstone tier the finding proposes.

Tier 1 (primary, in-operation): The divest/remove verb's revoke(all-held) must retry its own multi-scope revoke idempotently to completion, carrying the Class-C (403, empty-glob) propagation tolerance that memo-20260604's fix (commit 1a66443e: rbgi_revoke_repo_member, rbgi_revoke_sa_member) already established. Most half-failed revokes are transient 403-flaps that resolve within ~81s, so finishing the operation in place avoids ever producing a stranded fragment.

Tier 2 (audit, for a fully abandoned mid-divest, e.g. process killed): Add explicit handling so the audit can distinguish an interrupted-but-authorized revoke from out-of-band surplus. Concretely, record revoke intent before touching IAM — a roster "revoking" marker / tombstone — so the audit recognizes a fragment whose set was declared until this operator's in-flight revoke and finishes it idempotently, distinct from a genuine out-of-band surplus that must stay human-adjudicated. Equivalently, fix the ordering ambiguity: state revoke's roster-vs-IAM order explicitly (the memo states grant is roster-first at line 126 but is silent on revoke), and add the missing taxonomy row so surplus-fragment's likely-cause no longer reads "tampering / shrunk definition" alone.

Also reconcile the standing assertion at memo:116 ("the destructive operation is always human-driven / Never auto-revoke") with the routine auto-revoke in divest/remove (memo:50, 65) — as written they conflict, and the half-failed-revoke case is exactly where the conflict bites. Cross-reference memo-20260604's Class-C revoke tolerance as the reused propagation machinery.

findings.title: Roster-write invariant misdescribes auto-heal as a roster-write
findings.location: memo §The audit → "Invariant the asymmetry rests on" (lines 118-122)
findings.issue: The just-amended invariant says "Auto-converging deficits ... so writing the roster *upward* is itself a grant." This contradicts the memo's own deficit-heal definition. A deficit is IAM short of intent (A < D); healing it means re-running the grant to write IAM upward (line 111: "auto-converge up to D (re-run the idempotent grant)") — it does NOT write the roster. The roster already declares D; that is why the state is a deficit. Writing the roster upward would be changing intent, i.e. "auto-backfill intent," which line 115 says the system must NEVER do. So the invariant's stated mechanism ("auto-heal writes the roster") directly contradicts both the deficit definition (auto-heal writes IAM) and the surplus rule (never auto-backfill intent). The security conclusion (roster-write ≥ grant authority) is sound, but for the OTHER reason: whoever can edit the roster upward can INDUCE the next auto-converge to grant those bindings — roster-write is an indirect/deferred grant, not the auto-heal itself.
findings.evidence: Line 111: "auto-converge up to D (re-run the idempotent grant)"; line 115: "never auto-backfill intent (re-enters the tautology)"; line 119: "writing the roster *upward* is itself a grant. The auto-heal is safe only if roster-write authority is at least as protected as the grant verbs"
findings.suggested_fix: Rewrite the invariant to attribute the grant power to the roster EDITOR, not the auto-heal: "Because the auto-heal converges IAM up to whatever the roster declares, anyone who can raise a roster entry can induce the next auto-converge to grant those bindings to IAM. Roster-write is therefore an indirect grant path — so roster-write authority must be at least as protected as setIamPolicy." Drop the phrase "writing the roster upward is itself a grant" (the auto-heal writes IAM, not the roster).

findings.title: Writers rule + keyfile topology cinch + roster-write invariant cannot all hold
findings.location: memo §declared roster "Writers" (line 93) vs §Authority topology (lines 173-176) vs roster invariant (lines 118-122); paddock cinches (lines 126-129) + Open (lines 150-151)
findings.issue: Three settled statements collide in the keyfile/solo tier. (1) Writers rule: "The governor writes director/retriever entries." (2) Topology cinch: keyfile/solo "pull[s] grant authority to the payor" and "drop[s] or demote[s]" the standing governor (paddock cinch line 128-129). (3) Roster-write invariant: roster-write authority must be ≥ grant authority. If the governor is DROPPED, there is no governor to write director/retriever roster entries (the Writers rule names no fallback writer). If the governor is DEMOTED to non-privileged (it no longer holds grant authority), then a demoted governor that still writes the roster makes roster-write MORE accessible than grant — exactly the "softer escalation path" the invariant forbids. The three cannot be simultaneously true under the topology the keyfile tier cinches.
findings.evidence: Memo line 93: "The governor writes director/retriever entries"; line 174-175: "Keyfile/solo ... a standing owner-holding citizen buys nothing and is pure attack surface; drop or demote it"; line 120: "otherwise it is a softer escalation path than setIamPolicy"; paddock Open line 150-151: "Whether the keyfile tier drops the standing governor entirely or demotes it to non-privileged"
findings.suggested_fix: Make the Writers rule tier/topology-conditional: state that the roster writer for a given entry must be the holder of grant authority for that entry's capability-sets under the active topology — so in keyfile/solo (authority at the payor) the PAYOR writes director/retriever entries too, and the "governor writes director/retriever" form holds only when a privileged governor exists (federation/multi-admin). This also resolves the cinch-on-Open coupling below.

findings.title: Verb-dissolution table asserts "mantle is not special" unconditionally
findings.location: memo §Verb dissolution table (line 48) vs §Governor teardown leak (lines 207-211); paddock §Inherited concern (lines 98-99)
findings.issue: The dissolution table flattens mantle into the generic register — "mantle (governor) | add-citizen + grant(governor-set) | ... mantle is not special." But the governor-teardown section establishes that achieving that clean dissolution for the governor is the expensive, possibly-descoped piece: the idempotent governor (add-once + rekey, which is what makes mantle == add-citizen+grant) is "*not* free" — it needs a stable name replacing today's datestamped governor-YYYYMMDDHHmm plus a one-time migration, and the heat may descope the governor entirely (line 211; paddock line 99). If the governor is descoped, mantle stays a special datestamped delete+recreate verb — i.e. mantle IS special. The table presents a contingent outcome as an absolute.
findings.evidence: Memo line 48: "mantle is not special"; line 208-210: "The further idempotent-governor refinement ... is *not* free: it needs a *stable* governor name in place of today's datestamped governor-YYYYMMDDHHmm ... plus a one-time migration"; line 211: "Standalone leak fix (if the heat descopes the governor)"; paddock line 98-99: "an idempotent governor is a further, not-free refinement"
findings.suggested_fix: Qualify the table note: "mantle is not special *once the governor is made idempotent* (stable name); if the governor stays datestamped/delete+recreate, mantle remains a special verb — see Governor teardown leak." Or move the unconditional claim out of the table into prose that carries the contingency.

findings.title: Auto-converge is cinched but its safety depends on an unresolved Open (roster home)
findings.location: paddock §Cinched decisions (lines 126-127) + §Open (lines 148-149); memo lines 119-122
findings.issue: "Audit healing is asymmetric: auto-converge deficits" is listed as a cinched decision. Its safety, by the heat's own invariant, requires roster-write authority ≥ grant authority — a property guaranteed only by resolving the Open "where the declared roster physically lives ... a security boundary." So a cinched behavior (auto-converge) is unsafe until an Open lands with the right ACL. The paddock cross-references the Open ("see Open"), so the dependency is not hidden — but nothing sequences the cinch behind the Open, leaving a real hazard that auto-converge could be implemented before its enabling ACL exists, at which point it becomes the softer-than-setIamPolicy escalation path the invariant warns against.
findings.evidence: Paddock cinch line 126-127: "auto-converge deficits ... with auto-converge presuming roster-write is at least as protected as grant"; Open line 148-149: "Where the declared roster physically lives ... roster-write authority must be at least as protected as the grant verbs (the auto-heal invariant)"
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sequencing note to the auto-converge cinch: "auto-converge ships only after the roster-home Open lands with roster-write ACL ≥ grant authority; until then the audit reports deficits rather than auto-healing." This keeps the cinch but blocks the unsafe early implementation.

findings.title: Migration cinch "read grants from IAM" re-invokes the retired lossy-derivation it sits beside
findings.location: paddock §Cinched decisions migration (lines 132-133) vs §Retired cinches (lines 139-143); memo lines 76-79 (no migration section)
findings.issue: The migration cinch says "read grants from IAM, register citizens in the declared roster." The Retired-cinches block on the same page retires "Roster derives from IAM" precisely because "deriving from IAM is lossy and makes the intended-vs-actual audit a tautology," and the memo states a director set is "several bindings smeared across project + Mason SA + repo + bucket; partial and ambiguous matches become representable" (lines 76-78). Migration actually works only because legacy SAs still carry a role-bearing NAME prefix (director-…/retriever-…, confirmed in RBSDR roster spec regex), so the role — and thus the capability-set — is recoverable from the name, not by lossy reverse-mapping of bindings. The paddock never marks this distinction, so a mount-time reader can read "read grants from IAM" as licensing exactly the lossy derivation the adjacent retired cinch forbids. Compounding it: the memo (the mechanism doc the paddock points to) has NO migration section at all.
findings.evidence: Paddock line 132: "read grants from IAM, register citizens in the declared roster"; line 139-141: "'Roster derives from IAM ...' Retired: under decoupling, deriving from IAM is lossy and makes the intended-vs-actual audit a tautology"; memo line 76-78: "deriving the roster from IAM is lossy reverse-mapping ... partial and ambiguous matches become representable"
findings.suggested_fix: Reword the migration cinch to "derive each legacy citizen's role (and thus its capability-set) from the role-prefixed SA NAME — a one-time bootstrap that works precisely because legacy names still carry the prefix the standing audit cannot rely on — then register the citizen in the declared roster." Add a short migration paragraph to the memo (currently absent) covering this plus how grandfathered SAs interact with the orphan marker (next finding).

findings.title: Orphan-marker mechanism does not account for grandfathered legacy SAs
findings.location: memo §Orphan hazard and the identity marker (lines 136-147) vs paddock migration cinch (lines 132-133)
findings.issue: The orphan hazard rests on "With no name prefix and no SA labels, nothing marks an SA as RBK-managed" — so new per-person citizens get an rbk: tag/description marker, found by the orphan sweep. But migration grandfathers legacy director-…/retriever-… SAs that pre-date the marker: they ARE RBK-managed yet bear no rbk: marker. During the migration window both populations coexist ("let legacy names age out"). Neither memo nor paddock says whether migration stamps the marker onto grandfathered SAs or whether the orphan sweep falls back to name-prefix for them — and if it does fall back to name-prefix, the orphan-hazard's founding premise ("no name prefix") is undermined for exactly the live population that exists today.
findings.evidence: Memo line 138: "With no name prefix and no SA labels, nothing marks an SA as RBK-managed"; line 143-144: "description sentinel ... used only for the orphan-sweep"; paddock line 132-133: "let legacy director-…/retriever-… names age out"
findings.suggested_fix: Add one line to the orphan/migration treatment: either "migration stamps the rbk: marker onto each grandfathered SA so the orphan sweep is marker-uniform," or "the orphan sweep recognizes legacy role-prefixed names as a transitional second signal until they age out." State which, so the sweep's input domain is unambiguous.

result.reasoning: I read the memo's audit section (lines 96-134) and the cited lines directly rather than trusting the finding's quotes; they check out verbatim. Line 85: "Capability-set definitions are code." Lines 86-87: the audit compares actual IAM to the code-defined bindings of the current definition. Line 131: "A capability-set definition change decomposes into deficit-on-additions (auto-heal via roster-wide re-converge) ... no special versioning machinery." So a code change that *expands* a set's binding list does produce a roster-wide deficit that the audit auto-converges — silently granting the new binding to every holder of that set, with the same logged-not-gated treatment as a transient failed-grant retry. The finding's mechanism is sound. The decisive evidence is the memo's own internal inconsistency: at lines 118-122 it argues carefully that writing the roster *upward* is "itself a grant" and therefore roster-write authority must be at least as protected as setIamPolicy (a real security boundary). It pointedly does NOT extend that identical reasoning to definition expansion — even though a definition expansion is, by the very same logic, a grant applied automatically to every holder. The memo guards the data path (roster) but leaves the code path (definitions) explicitly ungated ("no special versioning machinery"), so the privilege-expansion trust boundary silently relocates from "who can setIamPolicy / write the roster" to "who can merge a one-line change to the capability-set definitions" — a shift the memo never names. This is not already-fixed: the header lists incorporated findings #1-5/#9/#11/#12, and lines 131-132 still carry the unqualified "auto-heal" / "no special versioning machinery" language. The one mitigation the memo could lean on — that landing a code change requires merge/review access, a stronger gate than setIamPolicy — is real and is why I rate this medium rather than high; but it is exactly the distinction the memo fails to draw, and a design-capture memo that is otherwise this rigorous about the Pale should draw it before the heat builds the auto-converge.
result.refined_fix: Extend the audit invariant (memo lines 118-122) and the consequence at line 131 to treat a capability-set definition *expansion* as a grant event, not routine deficit auto-heal. Concretely: (1) State that "who may edit capability-set definitions" is a first-class authority equal to grant/setIamPolicy authority, parallel to the roster-write-authority claim already made — a definition expansion grants new privilege to every holder of that set. (2) Make the audit distinguish two deficit sources rather than auto-converging all deficits identically: a per-identity deficit against an *unchanged* definition (a genuine failed/partial grant — safe to auto-heal, the original case) vs. a deficit that newly appears across *all* holders of a set simultaneously (the signature of a definition expansion). The memo can detect this cheaply — a definition bump shows as a synchronized roster-wide deficit absent on the prior audit, whereas a failed grant is isolated to one identity. (3) Gate the roster-wide case behind the same human adjudication the memo already requires for surplus-legitimization (lines 113-116), e.g. a one-time "definition expansion confirmation" on first apply, distinct from re-converging a transient grant failure. This keeps the memo's "no special versioning machinery" goal for the common case (unchanged-definition deficits still auto-heal) while closing the silent-privilege-grant path for the expansion case. The finding's proposed fix is correct in spirit; this refinement adds the concrete detection heuristic (synchronized roster-wide deficit) so the gate can be triggered automatically rather than relying on an operator to notice the definition changed.

result.reasoning:
I read the memo and the two cited specs. The finding's three load-bearing claims all check out against the source:

1. The divest decomposition is exactly "revoke(all held) + remove-citizen" (memo line 49), and "remove-citizen" is described only in SA/key terms — "remove = revoke-all then delete the SA" (line 63). The memo NEVER mentions deleting the roster row as part of teardown. Confirmed gap.

2. The audit auto-converges declared-but-absent deficits: "deficit-whole | a declared set wholly absent in A | ... | auto-converge" (line 104) and "Deficit is ours ... → auto-converge up to D (re-run the idempotent grant)" (lines 111-112). The audit domain is "roster-identities × set-defined bindings" (line 134) — it iterates over roster rows. Confirmed.

3. The memo states the grant-side invariant — "grant writes intent first" (line 126) — but states no dual for teardown. This is a genuine asymmetry in the very invariant the asymmetric-heal rests on (lines 118-122: declared roster = authoritative present intent). A surviving roster row after divest therefore presents as deficit and auto-converges.

So the hazard is real: if a divested identity's roster row survives, the audit re-grants its declared union. Because RBSDD's own completion note anticipates immediate re-invest of the same identity folio ("Proceed to director_invest to re-issue"), the acute case (same-name re-invest landing on a stale roster, silently inheriting the old capability union) is plausible and a privilege-persistence bug, not merely theoretical. The today's-divest spec (RBSDD) is fail-loud and touches no roster (rosters are read-only emission per RBSDR — confirmed: RBSDR is purely "read-only observation ... no service accounts are created or modified"), so the new model genuinely introduces a write-ordering obligation that the decomposition leaves unspecified.

This is NOT already in the text — the memo has no teardown-side intent-withdrawal invariant and no roster-delete step in divest.

The fix is sound: roster-first ordering is correct because the inverse window (roster deleted, IAM not yet revoked) produces only a transient surplus, and surplus is reported-not-auto-healed (lines 113-116), hence safe; whereas roster-last/never produces an auto-converging deficit, which is the dangerous direction. The finding's ordering reasoning is internally consistent with the memo's own asymmetry.

Severity high is defensible: the correctness of the asymmetric-heal design hinges on the roster being authoritative present intent, and the memo undermines its own central invariant by omitting the teardown write. It is design-capture (nothing built), but a soundness gap in the core invariant warrants high.

2. The Open already carries the exact ACL constraint and is tagged "a security boundary, not just storage" — and it sits under "Open — resolve WITHIN the heat" (mandatory in-heat, not deferrable).
3. The audit section (paddock 64-65) bridges them explicitly: "a security constraint on where the roster lives; see Open." The memo (88-92, 118-122) does the same.
4. Most decisively, the hazard is structurally impossible: auto-converge re-runs grants derived from the declared roster's D (memo 111-119, "writing the roster upward is itself a grant"; "re-run the idempotent grant"). Auto-converge has a hard DATA dependency on the roster's physical home. You cannot implement code that writes/reads a roster whose physical home (GCS object vs GAR artifact + etag discipline) is undecided. The roster-home Open is therefore a natural prerequisite of ALL roster-touching code, including auto-converge — the implementation ordering the finding wants is already forced by data dependency, not in need of prose.

The finding itself concedes "the dependency is not hidden." What it asks for — a "ships only after the roster-home Open lands" sequencing note — is pace/implementation ordering, exactly what JJK paddock posture and this heat explicitly defer ("Scoping/heat-chopping is deferred," paddock line 112; the prompt's own known-open caveat). result.reasoning:
I read the memo directly and verified all three cited statements. Line 93-94 (Writers): "The governor writes director/retriever entries; the payor writes the governor entry — matching the authority hierarchy." Lines 118-122 (invariant): "writing the roster *upward* is itself a grant... safe only if roster-write authority is at least as protected as the grant verbs (setIamPolicy); otherwise it is a softer escalation path than setIamPolicy." Lines 173-176 (topology): keyfile/solo → "pull grant authority to the payor where a standing governor buys nothing"; "a standing owner-holding citizen buys nothing and is pure attack surface; drop or demote it."

The collision is real and provable from the memo alone. The Authority section (149-183) is explicitly topology-dependent, but the Writers rule (93) is topology-blind — it hardcodes a standing privileged governor as the director/retriever roster writer. Under the keyfile/solo topology the heat's current tier actually uses, grant authority is pulled to the payor and the governor is dropped or demoted. Both branches break the rule: (drop) the Writers rule names no fallback writer, so director/retriever roster writes are undefined; (demote) a non-privileged governor still writing the roster makes roster-write more accessible than payor-held grant authority — exactly the "softer escalation path than setIamPolicy" the invariant at line 120 forbids. RBSDK-director_invest.adoc confirms the present model: the governor is the standing privileged actor that mints directors and smears their bindings across project/Mason/bucket/repo, consistent with the memo's own line 75. So the Writers rule encodes the federation/multi-admin topology, not the keyfile topology, and contradicts both the cinched authority direction and the security-load-bearing invariant.

Caveats that lower nothing material: (a) the finding calls the keyfile topology "cinched," but drop-vs-demote is an explicit open (memo line 238 / prompt). What's settled is the direction (authority at payor; governor is not the right tool in keyfile); the open status makes the fix more, not less, warranted, since both open branches break the rule. (b) I could not verify the finding's paddock line citations (paddock was passed as "undefined"), but the collision does not depend on them — it is fully established from memo lines 93, 118-122, 173-176, which I read directly. The suggested fix (make the Writers rule topology-conditional, writer == grant-authority holder for that entry's sets under the active topology) is correct and directly discharges the invariant by construction.
result.refined_fix: Make the Writers rule topology-conditional and bind it to the audit invariant at memo lines 118-122. Replace the flat line-93 statement with: "The roster writer for an entry must be the holder of grant authority for that entry's capability-sets under the active topology (this is the same invariant the audit's asymmetric heal rests on: roster-write authority == grant authority). Federation/multi-admin (standing privileged governor): the governor writes director/retriever entries, payor writes the governor entry. Keyfile/solo (grant authority pulled to the payor; governor dropped or demoted): the payor writes director/retriever entries directly — a demoted or absent governor must NOT write roster entries it lacks grant authority for, else roster-write becomes a softer escalation path than setIamPolicy." This resolves both open branches: under drop, the payor is the named fallback writer; under demote, the demoted governor is excluded from roster writes, preserving the invariant.

result.reasoning: I verified both halves independently. (1) The 12-logic-operator limit is REAL: GCP's linting-policies documentation defines a ConditionComplexityCheck with error "Logical operators count must not be more than 12," counting &&, ||, ! (confirmed via WebFetch of docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/linting-policies and the search of the IAM quotas/conditions docs). So the finding's external fact is accurate. (2) But the finding does NOT identify a real gap in the memo's design. The memo's prescribed governor condition (lines 156-163) is a single modifiedGrantsByRole hasOnly clause: `api.getAttribute(...).hasOnly([...])`. That expression contains ZERO logic operators — hasOnly() is a method call, not a logic operator, and the comma-separated role array is not a logic operator either. The 12-operator ceiling is therefore unreachable: the condition sits at 0 against a cap of 12. Crucially, the memo ALREADY states the binding constraint on line 162 ("don't join `hasOnly` with `&&`/`||`"), which is the actual Google modifiedGrantsByRole restriction and is STRICTER than the 12-operator cap — it forbids even one join. The finding's premise ("a real owner-replacement condition tends to accrue resource/date guards joined to the hasOnly") contradicts both that Google feature constraint and the memo's own already-present rule. The finding's own suggested_fix concedes this: "the no-&&/|| rule already pushes toward one hasOnly per binding." So the suggested addition would document a ceiling (12) that cannot be approached because a tighter ceiling (0 joins) is already in force and already written down. Per the codebase's load-bearing-complexity discipline, adding the 12-operator note carries no weight against the prescribed shape and would be noise. Real external fact, but not a real design gap.
result.refined_fix: No fix needed. The memo's already-present rule "don't join `hasOnly` with `&&`/`||`" (line 162) is the binding, stricter constraint: it pins the governor's modifiedGrantsByRole condition to a single operator-free hasOnly clause (zero logic operators), so the 12-logic-operator ConditionComplexityCheck ceiling is structurally unreachable and adding it to the limits list would be non-load-bearing noise. If the maintainer nonetheless wants the relationship recorded for a future federation-tier condition that legitimately combines guards on a DIFFERENT (non-modifiedGrantsByRole) binding, the accurate one-liner is: "GCP caps any single condition expression at 12 logic operators (&&/||/!) — irrelevant to the governor's hasOnly binding, which the no-join rule already pins to zero operators, but a budget to watch on resource/date-guarded federation conditions." That is a forward-looking note, not a correction to the citizen-tier governor design.

result.reasoning:
I independently verified each evidentiary claim against the source artifacts.

MEMO (Memos/memo-20260605-citizen-capability-model.md, read in full, 241 lines): Confirmed it has NO migration section. The only migration references are line 210 ("a one-time migration of existing datestamped governors" — governor-name migration) and line 236 ("the per-person migration"). Neither covers grandfathering legacy role-named operator SAs. Lines 75-78 confirm the memo's lossy-reverse-mapping argument: a director set is "several bindings smeared across project + Mason SA + repo + bucket; partial and ambiguous matches become representable." So the finding's memo quotes are accurate, and the no-migration-section claim is TRUE.

PADDOCK (.claude/jjm/jjp_uBuZ.md — the actual committed paddock, which the prompt supplied as "undefined"): The migration cinch is at lines 130-131 (finding said 132-133 — stale by ~2 lines), the retired cinch at lines 137-141 (finding said 139-143). Line-number drift is the only inaccuracy in the citations; durable content matches.

The migration cinch reads: "Migration grandfathers existing role-named SAs: read grants from IAM, register citizens in the declared roster, let legacy `director-…`/`retriever-…` names age out. No flag day." The retired cinch retires "Roster derives from IAM ... lossy ... tautology."

CULT-VERB SPECS: RBSDR roster spec confirms role is recovered from the SA NAME prefix via regex `^${RBCC_account_director}-(.+)@...` (lines 37, 45). RBSDK invest spec confirms director bindings are scattered across project/Mason-SA/bucket/repo (steps "Grant Project-Level / Mason Impersonation / build_bucket / repository Admin"). RBCC_account_* constants in rbgg_Governor.sh confirm name-prefix composition (`${RBCC_account_director}-${identity}`). So the finding's claim — migration works via the recoverable name prefix, not clean binding reverse-mapping — is technically correct.

WHERE THE FINDING OVERSTATES: It claims "the paddock never marks this distinction," but the migration cinch ITSELF says "grandfathers existing role-named SAs" and "let legacy director-…/retriever-… names age out" — the name-prefix mechanism is named in the very same sentence. So the distinction is partially marked already; what is missing is (a) the explicit statement that the role is recovered FROM THE NAME (the cinch's "read grants from IAM" muddies this) and (b) the one-time-bootstrap vs standing-derivation framing that cleanly resolves the surface tension with the retired cinch. The genuine, narrow defect is the phrase "read grants from IAM" sitting beside a retired cinch that forbids IAM-derivation, with no inline marker that this is a one-time populate-the-stored-roster step rather than the forbidden standing equivalence.

Net: the issue is real (a clarity hazard + a genuinely absent memo migration section), but the finding's framing ("never marks this distinction") is imprecise and its self-evidence partly overstated. Hence partial. Severity is low-to-medium — the cinch already grounds itself in role-named SAs, so this is polish on a mostly-correct cinch plus a real doc gap, not a design hole.
result.refined_fix:

Paddock migration cinch (jjp_uBuZ.md lines 130-131): replace the ambiguous "read grants from IAM" with language that names the recovery source and marks it as a one-time bootstrap, distinguishing it from the retired standing-derivation. This is not the retired 'roster derives from IAM' equivalence — it populates a stored, drift-able roster once, using the name prefix the standing audit cannot rely on; thereafter the roster stands independently. recommendations.theme: SA-naming migration is the unstated foundation (identity-only SA names)
recommendations.issue: The whole model rests on the SA name becoming identity-only (alice, not director-alice) — that is the precondition for 'one citizen = one SA = one key carrying the union' and for the lossy-IAM-roster justification — yet the memo never names the change for director/retriever or its downstream spec edits, even though it handled the analogous governor-name migration (lines 207-210). All four identity verbs hard-encode the role prefix (RBSDK/RBSRK compose ${RBCC_account_director}-<identity>, RBSDR filters/strips it, RBSDD synthesizes it, RBSRR defines three role-keyed RBRA paths).
recommendations.fix: Add a 'Citizen naming migration' subsection mirroring what lines 207-210 already do for the governor: (1) state the citizen SA name becomes identity-only; (2) enumerate the surfaces — RBSDK/RBSRK composition drops the prefix, RBSDD email synthesis becomes identity-only, RBSDR's prefix-filter roster is retired in favor of the declared-roster read, RBSRR's three role-keyed rbra.env paths collapse toward one identity-keyed path; (3) state existing-SA cutover: GCP cannot rename an SA, so director-*/retriever-* SAs migrate by delete-old + invest-new, re-entering the divest revoke-before-delete + the invest create/propagation race (the memo-20260604 churn), so it must run through the rbk-08 revoke layer + Class-C tolerance. Leave whether-this-heat-does-it under the existing open scoping flag (line 238).

recommendations.theme: Audit domain excludes non-roster principals — escalation via a fresh SA is invisible
recommendations.issue: Line 134 scopes the audit to 'roster-identities × set-defined bindings; non-roster principals are out of scope,' and the surplus taxonomy is computed per-roster-identity. But a leaked director key holds repo-scope setIamPolicy (roles/artifactregistry.repoAdmin per RBSDK) and can grant a powerful role to a FRESH principal the roster never names — which the audit never enumerates, so surplus never fires. The memo affirmatively claims this repoAdmin residual is 'exactly the resource-scope residual the detective audit covers' (lines 166-168), but an identity-first audit cannot see it.
recommendations.fix: Add a second, member-first audit axis: for every depot resource where an RBK principal can reach setIamPolicy — the AR repo, the build bucket, and individual SAs — enumerate ACTUAL members and flag any RBK-relevant binding whose member is neither a roster identity nor an expected system identity (Mason, payor/governor, future Envoy, gated by an allowlist to avoid false positives). State the audit must walk policies member-first as well as roster-first. Correct line 134 so resource-policy MEMBERS are in scope even when not roster identities, and soften lines 166-168's 'covers' claim to 'covers only once this member-first axis exists.'

recommendations.theme: Divest omits the roster write — auto-converge resurrects a torn-down citizen
recommendations.issue: Verb dissolution decomposes divest into 'revoke(all held) + remove-citizen' (line 49) and remove as 'revoke-all then delete the SA' (line 63), never mentioning deletion of the roster row, and the ordering is unspecified. If a divested identity's roster row survives, the audit reads declared-but-absent = deficit and auto-converges (lines 104-112), silently re-granting capabilities to the identity just torn down — acute because RBSDD anticipates immediate same-folio re-invest, so a stale roster would silently re-confer the old capability union on the freshly re-issued SA. The memo states the grant-side invariant ('grant writes intent first', line 126) but no teardown-side dual.
recommendations.fix: Add the teardown dual of 'grant writes intent first': specify remove-citizen as (1) withdraw intent — delete the identity's roster row(s) — FIRST, (2) then revoke IAM, (3) then delete the SA. State the invariant: 'no IAM revoke without a prior-or-atomic roster withdrawal.' Justify by the audit's own asymmetry — roster-first leaves only a transient surplus (reported, never auto-healed), which is safe; roster-last/never leaves an auto-converging deficit.

recommendations.theme: 'citizen' collides with and inverts RBSHR's existing 'citizen' (artifact)
recommendations.issue: The memo mints 'citizen' = operator IDENTITY (a person), but RBSHR already uses 'citizen' for a depot-resident ARTIFACT (property) — hoard line 97 'A {rbtgi_depot} citizen alongside the {rbtga_ark}' and bullion line 222 'bullions are the new AWS-side citizen' — the opposite category. Preferred: keep 'citizen' for the operator identity and reword RBSHR's two prose uses away from 'citizen' (e.g. '{rbtgi_depot}-resident artifact'; 'AWS-side artifact/deliverable'); add a memo note 'supersedes RBSHR prose citizen (artifact) usage' paralleling the memo-20260527 divergence note. recommendations.theme: 'declared roster' overloads the existing roster cult-verb (actual vs intent)
recommendations.issue: The memo mints 'declared roster' = stored INTENT, but RBK's existing roster cult-verb (RBSDR) emits ACTUAL discovered IAM state ('one roster-director fact file per discovered identity'). The verb-dissolution table (line 50) maps 'roster | read the declared roster,' silently retargeting the actual-reading verb onto the intent noun — the two are opposite poles of the very intent-vs-actual axis the audit is built on. recommendations.theme: Capability-set definition EXPANSION is auto-applied as a silent privilege grant
recommendations.issue: The memo treats a capability-set definition change as routine 'deficit-on-additions, auto-heal ... no special versioning machinery' (line 131). But definitions are code (line 85), and an expansion (adding a binding) turns every holder of that set into a deficit the audit auto-converges — granting the new binding to all holders automatically, with the same ungated treatment as a failed-grant retry. This silently relocates the privilege-expansion trust boundary from 'who can setIamPolicy / write the roster' (which the memo carefully guards at lines 118-122) to 'who can merge a one-line definition change' — a boundary the memo never names.

Distinguish two deficit sources rather than auto-converging all identically — a per-identity deficit against an UNCHANGED definition (a genuine failed grant, safe to auto-heal) vs a deficit appearing across ALL holders of a set simultaneously (the signature of a definition expansion). recommendations.theme: 10-key-per-SA quota unaddressed against the rekey lifecycle
recommendations.issue: The model introduces rekey as a first-class verb ('rotate the key, bindings untouched', line 63) and the whole pitch is freely decoupled key rotation — but GCP enforces a hard, non-adjustable 10-keys-per-SA limit. A rekey that fails partway (create ok, delete fails — the orphan-hazard class) or that does not reclaim old keys accumulates toward the ceiling; the 11th errors. The memo never states the create-vs-delete ordering for rekey nor acknowledges the quota, despite already maintaining a GCP-constraints 'Verified facts' section.

Do NOT mandate delete-before-create (it risks leaving an active citizen keyless if create fails after delete); specify the availability-safe order: create-new, deliver RBRA, verify, delete-old. Add a stale-key reclaim step, and extend the orphan/audit machinery to detect surplus keys on a tracked SA (>1 user-managed key where one is expected), so a stranded key is both findable and quota-reclaimable.

recommendations.theme: Concurrent grant vs audit auto-converge can fight; revoke ordering unspecified
recommendations.issue: Two writers to IAM run concurrently: the grant verb and the audit's deficit auto-converge. The memo states grant ordering (roster-first, line 126) but is silent on revoke ordering and on whether auto-converge reads the roster fresh. Concrete race: a revoke removes the roster entry, a concurrent audit that already read the OLD roster auto-converges the just-revoked set back into IAM — silently re-granting what an operator just revoked. The asymmetric-healing reasoning addresses surplus but not this deficit-side resurrection-by-stale-read.

recommendations.theme: Orphan marker doesn't account for grandfathered legacy SAs during migration
recommendations.issue: The orphan hazard rests on 'no name prefix and no SA labels, nothing marks an SA as RBK-managed' (line 138), so new citizens get an rbk: marker found by the sweep. But migration grandfathers legacy director-…/retriever-… SAs that are RBK-managed yet bear no marker, and both populations coexist during the age-out window. Neither memo nor paddock says whether migration stamps the marker onto grandfathered SAs or whether the sweep falls back to name-prefix — and a name-prefix fallback would resurrect the lossy derivation the model retires.
recommendations.fix: Add one line: migration stamps the rbk: marker onto each grandfathered legacy SA so the orphan sweep operates on a marker-uniform input domain (prefer this over a name-prefix fallback, which contradicts the retired lossy-derivation). State that until the marker is back-stamped the sweep's coverage of the legacy population is incomplete, making the back-stamp a migration prerequisite.

recommendations.theme: Ephemeral governor 'shrinks resting exposure' only of the binding, not the key
recommendations.issue: The ephemeral-governor option (lines 181-183) — 'grant the governor-set for one op, then revoke' — claims it 'shrinks resting owner-exposure.' But in the keyfile tier the governor is an SA with an RBRA key on disk (RBSGM), and capability-revoke is decoupled from key-destruction (lines 59-66), so revoking the governor-set strips the binding but leaves the key on disk. It shrinks only the binding, not the keyfile tier's actual attack surface (the file to steal); during grant->use->revoke and any revoke lag (~81s, memo-20260604) the on-disk key carries full owner.
recommendations.fix: Add a caveat to the ephemeral-governor bullet: because capability-revoke and key-destruction are decoupled, 'shrinks resting owner-exposure' shrinks only the binding — to reduce the keyfile attack surface, couple key-destruction (rekey-to-nothing or SA delete) to the revoke; and note the grant->use->revoke window plus revoke lag. Do not add the 'circular setIamPolicy' framing (the payor is the grant authority in keyfile/solo) and do not re-label the option (already hedged to 'relevant only if a keyfile delegated governor is ever wanted').

recommendations.theme: Mechanism leak: GCP-primitive justification in the paddock spine bullet
recommendations.issue: The paddock spine bullet (lines 32-35) inlines '; this is native to the GCP primitive (an SA may exist with zero bindings)' — an implementation justification for why decoupling is achievable, i.e. mechanism. The paddock cinches 'shape, not mechanism' (line 3) and elsewhere consistently pushes the why to the memo via pointers; this most-load-bearing bullet breaks that pattern.
recommendations.fix: Drop the entire substrate clause (not just the parenthetical — 'this is native to the GCP primitive' is itself the leaking justification): 'Adding/removing a citizen is independent of granting/revoking its capabilities. Today's invest fuses the two; the model unfuses them.' The zero-bindings fact is already carried by the memo's Identity-verbs section. If a pointer is wanted, append '(why this is achievable: memo).'

recommendations.theme: Vocabulary defined twice — paddock re-defines terms the memo owns
recommendations.issue: Citizen / capability-set / ontology definitions are spelled out in both the paddock (lines 27-47) and the memo (lines 16-31), and the core noun-phrases already diverge slightly (paddock 'multi-role is first-class'; memo 'possession of the key is the identity'), so a vocabulary edit must land twice or drift. The paddock is the orientation surface and must stay self-standing, so it can't strip terms to bare names — but the duplicated definitional clauses can compress.
recommendations.fix: Keep every shape CLAIM (rank-neutral, multi-role-first-class, ontology). Compress only the redundant core definitional noun-phrases so the memo's Scope-and-vocabulary stays the single owner (e.g. drop 'a depot-issued SA holding one RBRA key' from the paddock citizen bullet) and add one back-pointer '(definitions: memo)' mirroring the pattern the paddock already uses elsewhere.

recommendations.theme: Auto-heal roster-write invariant stated three times in the paddock
recommendations.issue: The invariant 'roster-write authority >= grant verbs' appears three times in the paddock: in the audit prose (with rationale), in the Open item, and in the cinch bullet. One full statement plus pointers suffices.
recommendations.fix: Keep the audit-prose statement intact — it carries the load-bearing rationale ('else it is a softer escalation path') and already ends with 'see Open.' Drop the trailing parenthetical from the cinch bullet, and in the Open item shorten the restated invariant to a back-reference (keep 'a security boundary, not just storage'; replace the restatement with '(the auto-heal invariant; see The audit)').

recommendations.theme: Federate noun sits beside RBSHR's 'federates' verb across the Workforce/Workload seam
recommendations.issue: The memo's peer noun 'federate' (Workforce-federated operator) reads cleanly against memo-20260527, but RBSHR uses 'federates' as a verb for the envoy (Workload/machine egress, lines 213/238) — the two sit one grep apart on the very Workforce/Workload seam the project takes pains to separate. RBSHR line 316 and memo-20260527 already draw the seam, but the new memo does not point to it.
recommendations.fix: Append a one-clause pointer to the Federate bullet: '(Workforce-federated operator subject; not the envoy's Workload federates-to-AWS egress, which RBSHR Envoy and memo-20260527 Workforce-vs-Workload already separate).' Point to the existing disambiguation rather than re-deriving it; do not assert 'unrelated' without naming where the seam is drawn.

recommendations.theme: What-done-looks-like carries a redundant verb-mapping parenthetical
recommendations.issue: The paddock 'What done looks like' parenthetical '(invest/divest/roster/mantle -> add/grant/revoke/remove + read, parameterized by capability-set)' restates the cinched verb dissolution already present at the cinch line and in the memo table. (The six-surface list and the load-bearing-core sentence are legitimate paddock 'what done looks like' shape — durable domain entry points, not stale file:line enumerations — and should stay.)
recommendations.fix: Trim only the redundant verb-mapping parenthetical to '(the admin verbs collapse to the dissolved set)' or drop it, since the dissolution is cinched elsewhere. Keep the six-surface scope and the identity-keyed-accessor core sentence; keep the 'generalizing memo-20260527 single code seam' pointer (a compliant pointer).

recommendations.theme: Committed memo-20260527 carries no forward breadcrumb to the active redesign
recommendations.issue: memo-20260527's Status block still reads 'Committed plan' with no pointer to the citizen redesign, and RBSHR cites it as authoritative — so a reader landing there first misses that the keyfile-tier shape is under revision. The memo deliberately defers the full divergence note to 'when the model is frozen' (line 240), so a dated supersede note now would contradict that cinched deferral.
recommendations.fix: Add a minimal, deferral-honoring breadcrumb near the Status line (NOT the full supersede note): 'NOTE (2026-06-05): the keyfile-tier shape is under active redesign in heat BZ (memo-20260605-citizen-capability-model.md); the one-SA-per-role and verb-naming assumptions here may change. Not yet revised — the citizen memo will fold its divergences in once that model is frozen.' This closes the read-from-here-first gap without presuming the redesign is settled.

result.overall: This is a strong, unusually rigorous design-capture pair: the paddock holds shape cleanly with consistent memo-pointers, and the memo is candid about its open questions and even self-incorporated a prior review pass. The findings that survived verification fall into three tiers. The most valuable are real soundness gaps that undermine the model's own central claims: (1) the SA-naming migration to identity-only names is the unstated foundation the whole one-SA-per-person/union-key thesis rests on, yet only the governor's analogous migration is spelled out; (2) the audit affirmatively claims to cover the repoAdmin setIamPolicy residual but, being roster-identity-first, cannot see escalation onto a fresh non-roster principal; and (3) divest never deletes the roster row, so auto-converge would resurrect a torn-down citizen — a direct contradiction of the asymmetric-heal invariant the design is built on. A middle tier of vocabulary collisions (citizen vs RBSHR's artifact-citizen; declared-roster vs the actual-reading roster verb) are genuine within-domain monosemy hazards against committed text and warrant operator judgment before the terms freeze, since they touch RBSHR. The remaining security refinements (definition-expansion auto-grant, 10-key quota, concurrency/revoke ordering, half-failed revoke classification, forgeable orphan marker, the depot-bucket home violating its own invariant) are each real and cheap to fold in. The low-severity items are mostly paddock/memo hygiene — duplication, one mechanism leak, contingent claims stated absolutely — safe to apply now. Note that several findings the original reviewers rated high were correctly de-rated by verification because the memo already flags the issue as open or already hedges the option; I have preserved those de-ratings.
ordered_actions: Add a 'Citizen naming migration' subsection to the memo (identity-only SA names; enumerate RBSDK/RBSRK/RBSDD/RBSDR/RBSRR surfaces; existing-SA delete+recreate cutover via the rbk-08 revoke layer + Class-C tolerance) — the foundation both the union-key claim and the lossy-roster justification rest on.

Add the member-first audit axis (enumerate actual members on repo/bucket/SA policies, flag non-roster/non-system members) and correct line 134 + soften lines 166-168 — closes the invisible-escalation-via-fresh-principal gap the memo claims it covers.

Specify divest/remove ordering: withdraw roster intent FIRST, then revoke IAM, then delete SA; state the 'no IAM revoke without prior roster withdrawal' dual of grant-writes-intent-first — prevents auto-converge resurrecting a torn-down citizen.
Decide and resolve the 'citizen' term collision with RBSHR (reword RBSHR's artifact-citizen prose OR pick a different identity noun; add a memo divergence note) before the term freezes.
Decide and resolve the 'declared roster' overload of the actual-reading roster cult-verb (preferred: rename the intent store to a 'declared ledger') before the term freezes.

Fold in the definition-expansion auto-grant gate, the 10-key-per-SA rekey constraint, the revoke-ordering/concurrency contract, the half-failed-revoke taxonomy row + in-operation retry, and the integrity-advisory orphan-sweep cross-check — five cheap memo additions.

Note in the 'Physical home (open)' bullet that the depot bucket (= build bucket, director holds objectCreator) fails the roster-write invariant; require a governor/payor-only write ACL and cinch 'roster-write is never a capability-set member.'
Make the Writers rule topology-conditional (writer == grant-authority holder under the active topology) to discharge the keyfile drop/demote collision.

Apply the paddock/memo hygiene set: drop the GCP-primitive mechanism leak from the spine bullet; compress the duplicated vocabulary definitions; de-duplicate the thrice-stated roster-write invariant; trim the redundant verb-mapping parenthetical; qualify 'mantle is not special'; reword the migration cinch + add the orphan/grandfather line; add the ephemeral-governor key-coupling caveat and the federate/Workload pointer.
Add a minimal deferral-honoring breadcrumb to memo-20260527 pointing at the active redesign (without the full supersede note, honoring the 'record when frozen' deferral).
